title: Expand strlen_strict conformance: longer strings, high bytes, hardened mode
description: Add fixture cases for strlen edge cases and hardened mode. Current: 3 cases (strict only). Target: +6 cases covering longer strings and hardened mode.

title: test: timerfd nonblock keeps cloexec clear
close_reason: Completed timerfd nonblock/cloexec parity coverage

title: detect_scanf_type misparses suppressed bracket matches
description: `detect_scanf_type` in `frankenlibc_conformance/src/lib.rs` uses `while chars.next().is_some_and(|c| !c.is_alphabetic() && c != '[') {}` to skip suppressed specifiers (`%*`). However, if the suppressed specifier is a bracket match like `%*[^%]`, the loop terminates early on the `[` character. The remaining characters (`^%]`) are then parsed by the outer loop, which incorrectly identifies the `%` inside the bracket expression as the start of a new format specifier. This can cause the test harness to allocate an incorrect number of output variables for `sscanf` cases.
close_reason: Fixed misparsing of suppressed bracket matches in detect_scanf_type.

title: Expand dirent_ops conformance: rewinddir, seekdir, telldir, dirfd
description: Add executors and fixture cases for additional POSIX directory functions. Current coverage: 6 cases. Target: +8 cases.

title: fnmatch: unterminated '[' bracket should be treated as literal (matches glibc)
description: fnmatch_impl in crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/string_abi.rs returns false when a '[' bracket expression hits NUL without a closing ']'. Glibc treats an unterminated bracket as a literal '[' character (POSIX leaves this undefined; glibc's choice is the de-facto contract). Surfaced by fuzz_pattern_match: pattern='**[' (collapses to '*['), string='[', flags=FNM_NOESCAPE|FNM_CASEFOLD: ours returns 1 (no match), glibc returns 0 (match). Fix: pre-scan the bracket for a closing ']' before NUL; if absent, fall through to literal-byte comparison.
close_reason: classify_bracket() now reproduces glibc's three-way handling of unterminated [ ŌĆö Terminated/LiteralFallback/Invalid. fnmatch_impl also resets at_start to false whenever any string char is consumed, fixing a leading-period false-positive surfaced by the same fuzz run. Three regression tests added; 180s fuzz pass with 35K execs found no further divergences. Committed 40e2a69.

Add conformance-matrix-case execution for the scanf fixture family, assert expected return/value contracts by mode, and fix any matrix-path regressions the new executable coverage surfaces.

title: bd-66wz subtask: Arena quarantine drain invariants + regression tests
description:
Background
- The membrane arena uses a quarantine queue to detect UAF while bounding memory retention.
- If quarantine drain semantics are wrong, we either: (a) lose UAF detection prematurely, (b) leak/quasi-leak freed metadata, or (c) fail under adversarial free storms.

Scope
- Add targeted regression tests in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/arena.rs` for quarantine drain behavior and metadata lifecycle invariants.
- Tests must be deterministic, fast, and failure messages must identify the violated invariant and the sequence that triggered it.

Key Invariants (must be asserted explicitly)
1) Quarantine max-bytes bound: when total quarantined bytes would exceed `QUARANTINE_MAX_BYTES`, the arena deterministically drains/evicts the oldest entries until within budget.
2) Generation safety: drained/evicted entries must not re-validate as live allocations.
3) Metadata hygiene: after drain, allocation headers/canary bookkeeping must not allow stale pointers to validate as Writable/Valid.
4) Idempotence: repeated drain triggers under the same sequence cannot oscillate internal counters.

Test Plan
- Construct a deterministic sequence of alloc/free operations that exceeds the quarantine byte budget (use a small number of large allocations to cross the threshold quickly).
- Assert: drain occurs; arena lookup for freed pointers yields a restrictive state (Freed/Invalid/Quarantined as appropriate) and never Valid/Writable.
- Assert: quarantine accounting counters are non-negative and monotone w.r.t. operations.

Acceptance
- New unit tests in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/arena.rs` cover the above invariants and pass under `cargo test -p frankenlibc-membrane`.
- Tests complete quickly (<1s locally) and are deterministic (no time dependence, no RNG without fixed seed).
- Failure output includes: invariant_id, step index, alloc size, and pointer class (live/freed/foreign).

Notes
- This bead is a pure test+invariant closure; do not change arena behavior unless a test demonstrates a real bug.
close_reason: Added quarantine drain invariants/regression test

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone

Implemented deterministic quarantine drain invariant coverage.

    - FIFO eviction order (oldest drained first)
    - quarantine_bytes within `QUARANTINE_MAX_BYTES` after drain

title: bd-66wz subtask: TLS validation cache epoch invalidation regression tests
description:
Background
- The TLS validation cache is a hot-path speed feature; correctness depends on preventing stale CachedValid hits after global validity changes (free/quarantine/generation bumps).
- The design uses a global epoch (`GLOBAL_TLS_CACHE_EPOCH`) to invalidate cache entries across threads.

Scope
- Add unit tests in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/tls_cache.rs` that explicitly exercise the epoch bump path and prove stale hits cannot survive an epoch change.

Key Invariants
1) Epoch mismatch forces miss: an entry inserted at epoch E must not hit when the current epoch is E+1 (or any !=E).
2) Epoch mismatch self-cleans: when addr matches but epoch mismatches, the entry is invalidated to avoid repeated epoch checks.
3) Hits/misses counters reflect behavior (hit does not increment on epoch mismatch).

Test Plan
- Insert a known entry; assert lookup hits.
- Call `bump_tls_cache_epoch()`; assert lookup for same addr misses and entry invalidates (follow-up lookup still misses without reinsert).
- Optionally: ensure bump affects caches across independent `TlsValidationCache` instances (epoch is global, not per-cache).

Acceptance
- New tests in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/tls_cache.rs` cover the above invariants and pass deterministically.
- Tests include clear assertion messages that point to the invariant being validated.

Notes
- Do not add non-deterministic timing; this is purely functional correctness.
close_reason: Added epoch invalidation regression tests + deterministic test lock

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone

  - Added test-only `TLS_CACHE_EPOCH_TEST_LOCK` + `lock_tls_cache_epoch_for_tests()` to prevent flaky expectations when other tests bump the global epoch via allocator frees.
  - Added `epoch_bump_invalidates_entry_and_self_cleans` asserting: hit at same epoch, miss after bump, self-clean invalidation, and hit after reinsert.
  - Wrapped existing hit/invalidate tests with the epoch lock to keep them deterministic under parallel test execution.

Verification

title: bd-66wz subtask: Pointer validation adversarial pipeline tests (free/UAF/canary/foreign)
description:
Background
- The `ValidationPipeline` is the TSM enforcement surface: its outcomes drive Allow/Repair/Deny decisions in libc entrypoints.
- We need direct adversarial regression tests proving the pipeline never returns permissive abstractions for non-live pointers, even when caches and heuristics are involved.

Scope
- Expand unit tests in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/ptr_validator.rs` (and, where appropriate, `arena.rs` / `fingerprint.rs`) to cover adversarial sequences and edge outcomes.
- Prefer testing via `ValidationPipeline::{allocate, free, validate}` instead of reaching into `arena` directly, so the page-oracle/bloom/TLS-cache integration is exercised.

Required Adversarial Cases
1) Foreign pointer validate: returns `Foreign(_)`, and must never claim bounded Valid/Writable remaining-bytes.
2) Foreign free: `ValidationPipeline::free()` must return `FreeResult::ForeignPointer` (and must not crash).
3) Double free: first free => `Freed` (or `FreedWithCanaryCorruption` if provoked); second free => `DoubleFree`.
4) UAF via generation: after free, `validate(addr)` must not yield `CachedValid(_)` and must yield a restrictive outcome (`TemporalViolation` or non-readable).
5) Canary corruption path: corrupt trailing canary; free returns `FreedWithCanaryCorruption`; subsequent validate for addr must remain non-readable/non-writable.
6) Cache interaction: populate TLS cache with a valid pointer; free it; validate again must miss cache and must not return a live abstraction.

Safety/Lattice Assertions
- For each case above, assert `can_read/can_write` matches expectation and that the extracted `PointerAbstraction` state is not `Valid/Writable` for freed/quarantined pointers.

Acceptance
- New tests added to `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/ptr_validator.rs` are deterministic, fast, and produce helpful failure context (case_id, sequence step).
- No new unsafe code outside the existing test intentional-overflow blocks.

Notes
- This bead is test-first: if a test fails, fix the pipeline/arena behavior with the smallest correct change and add a regression assertion.
close_reason: Added ptr validation adversarial tests (foreign/free/doublefree/canary) + stabilized cache-hit assertions

  - Switched allocation/free tests to exercise `ValidationPipeline::{allocate, free}` to cover page-oracle/bloom integration.
  - Added tests: `foreign_free_reported`, `double_free_reported`, `canary_corruption_detected_via_pipeline_free` (with local `#[allow(unsafe_code)]` + `// SAFETY:` comment).

Verification

title: bd-66wz subtask: Deterministic property/invariant tests for allocator+membrane state transitions
description:
Background
- Unit tests cover specific sequences; we also need broad invariant pressure over many sequences to catch edge transitions (especially around cache invalidation and quarantine accounting).
- We require determinism (replayable seeds) and short runtime so these can run in CI on every change.

Scope
- Add deterministic property-style tests (no external RNG dependency required) for allocator+membrane state transitions.
- Prefer a tiny local PRNG (e.g. xorshift64*) with fixed seeds so failures are reproducible without environment variables.

Invariant Set (examples; encode as named invariants in assertions)
1) No stale-permissive cache: after any operation that frees an allocation, subsequent `validate(addr)` must not return `CachedValid(_)` for that addr unless a new allocation was performed and revalidated.
2) Quarantine accounting soundness: quarantine_bytes and entry counts never go negative; bounded by max constraints after drain.
3) Temporal safety while tracked: if an allocation has been freed and is still tracked in quarantine/arena, `can_read/can_write` for that addr is false.
4) Determinism: given the same seed and step count, the sequence of operations and observed outcomes is identical.

Test Plan
- Generate N sequences across a small set of fixed seeds.
- Each step randomly chooses: allocate(size), free(ptr), validate(ptr), corrupt_canary(ptr) (guarded by ptr live), etc.
- Track a simple shadow model for expected permissiveness (live vs freed-tracked vs foreign) and assert pipeline outcomes respect it.

Acceptance
- Property/invariant tests live in `crates/frankenlibc-membrane` (module choice: `arena.rs`/`ptr_validator.rs`/new `tests/` module) and run under `cargo test -p frankenlibc-membrane`.
- Runtime is bounded and deterministic (<2s total locally).
- On failure, output includes: seed, step index, op, ptr addr, expected class, actual outcome.

Notes
- If adding a crate like `proptest` is considered later, keep this deterministic harness as the baseline (proptest can be an additive layer, not a replacement).
close_reason: Added deterministic invariant sequence property tests

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
Implemented deterministic property/invariant sequence pressure tests without adding external RNG dependencies.

Changes
- Added integration test: `crates/frankenlibc-membrane/tests/allocator_membrane_invariants_sequences_test.rs`
  - Deterministic xorshift64* PRNG with fixed seeds.
  - Runs bounded sequences (alloc/free/validate/double-free) over a small slot pool and asserts core invariants:
    - live pointers validate as `CachedValid`/`Validated` and are readable+writable
    - freed-but-tracked pointers validate as `TemporalViolation` and are not readable+writable
    - second free reports `DoubleFree`
    - foreign pointers validate as `Foreign` with `SafetyState::Unknown` and no claimed bounds
  - Runtime bounded (~0.2s) and failure context includes seed/step/op.

Verification
- `TMPDIR=$PWD/.tmp cargo test -p frankenlibc-membrane -- --nocapture` (unit + integration tests pass).

title: [HIGH] pthread_cond_init rejects a host-initialized pthread_condattr_t with EINVAL (2 tests deterministically fail)
description:
## Symptom

`cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_cond_core_test` has two deterministic failures:

```
test condvar_init_accepts_initialized_attr ... FAILED
test condvar_init_accepts_monotonic_attr_clock ... FAILED

thread 'condvar_init_accepts_initialized_attr' panicked at crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_cond_core_test.rs:86:9:
assertion `left == right` failed
  left: 22
 right: 0

thread 'condvar_init_accepts_monotonic_attr_clock' panicked at crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_cond_core_test.rs:107:9:
assertion `left == right` failed
  left: 22
 right: 0

test result: FAILED. 16 passed; 2 failed
```

rc=22 is EINVAL. Our `pthread_cond_init` is rejecting the `pthread_condattr_t` pointer with EINVAL where the test expects success (rc=0).

## Test bodies (authoritative ŌĆö not me)

crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_cond_core_test.rs:80 (condvar_init_accepts_initialized_attr):
```rust
let mut attr: libc::pthread_condattr_t = unsafe { std::mem::zeroed() };
assert_eq!(libc::pthread_condattr_init(&mut attr), 0);          // HOST libc init
assert_eq!(pthread_cond_init(cond, &attr), 0);                  // OURS ŌĆö FAILS here, got 22
assert_eq!(pthread_cond_destroy(cond), 0);
assert_eq!(libc::pthread_condattr_destroy(&mut attr), 0);
```

crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_cond_core_test.rs:96 (condvar_init_accepts_monotonic_attr_clock): same pattern, plus libc::pthread_condattr_setclock(&mut attr, CLOCK_MONOTONIC).

## Diagnosis

The tests use HOST libc's `pthread_condattr_init` / `pthread_condattr_setclock` to populate `attr`, then pass that `attr` pointer to OUR `pthread_cond_init`. Our impl validates the incoming attr against its own expected layout / sentinel bytes and returns EINVAL because the host-initialized bytes don't match.

This is a cross-impl boundary question rather than a pure regression ŌĆö the test fixture deliberately mixes host-initialized attrs with our cond_init because that is the real-world LD_PRELOAD pattern: user code links against host libc's condattr_init at compile time but our pthread_cond_init interposes at link time. If our cond_init rejects host-initialized condattrs in production, every real user program breaks under FRANKENLIBC_MODE=hardened.

## Fix direction

Two options:

1. **Relax validation**: treat any non-null `attr` pointer as acceptable, pull the clock / pshared via portable accessors (`pthread_condattr_getclock` / `getpshared`) instead of inspecting our own layout. This matches glibc's own behavior ŌĆö glibc never rejects a validly-constructed condattr of either origin.
2. **Pass-through**: when the attr's layout doesn't match our internal format, fall through to host `pthread_cond_init` the same way bd-21ypi (138c07c3) routes non-native pthread_t handles. Consistent with the existing host-handoff pattern.

Option 1 is simpler; option 2 matches the existing architectural pattern for 'didn't originate in us'.

## Provenance

Found by cc-libc FULL REVIEW MODE at HEAD 6c0380b4 br-bd-qhnxc: dispatch host pthread cancellation. The bd-qhnxc fix unblocked pthread_abi_test, which let cargo test --workspace --all-targets get further than before and expose this second pthread-cluster failure. Fix commits previously landing on the condvar path: 2e1d65bc 'test(abi): expand pthread condvar core tests', d7b4a832 'expand pthread condvar and thread lifecycle test coverage' ŌĆö so the tests are intentional coverage, not accidental.

## Blast radius

Two deterministic failures in crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_cond_core_test.rs. Blocks frankenlibc-abi integration-test suite from passing. Doesn't trip any SIGABRT or memory corruption.
close_reason: Accepted host-initialized pthread_condattr_t via host getclock fallback; workspace now advances to glibc_internal_abi_test allocator abort

title: PORT: getenv_r (NetBSD re-entrant getenv)
description: Port the NetBSD libc `getenv_r(name, buf, buflen)` re-entrant environment lookup. Looks up the value of the environment variable `name` and copies it into the caller-provided `buf` (which must hold at least `strlen(value)+1` bytes). Returns 0 on success, -1 with errno=ENOENT if the variable is not set, or -1 with errno=ERANGE if `buf` is too small for the value plus NUL. Useful for callers that want to avoid the global-pointer aliasing risk of plain getenv. Implementation: thin abi shim around the existing getenv() that copies bytes into the caller buffer with bounds checking; tests cover lookup-success, missing-variable, undersized-buffer, NULL-name/buf guards.

title: EPIC: Runtime Hot-Path Perf Gates (strict <20ns, hardened <200ns)
description:
Goal: enforce extreme-software-optimization discipline for runtime_math.

Why:
- runtime_math is on the membrane hot path. Any regression here leaks into every libc call we gate.

Constraints:
- Strict mode must stay <20ns/call overhead target.
- Hardened mode must stay <200ns/call overhead target.
- No behavior regressions without explicit isomorphism proof + fixture evidence.

Outputs:
- Microbench + profile baselines for RuntimeMathKernel::decide + observe_validation_result.
- Golden snapshot outputs to detect accidental semantic drift.
- A repeatable baseline/profile/verify loop that future work MUST follow.
close_reason: done

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text:
## Epic Notes: Extreme-Optimization Discipline For runtime_math

This epic encodes the non-negotiable loop: **baseline -> profile -> prove -> implement -> verify -> repeat**.

### What We Are Protecting
runtime_math runs at the ABI boundary of libc call paths. Any accidental overhead shows up everywhere.

Budgets (from AGENTS.md):
- strict overhead target: <20ns/call (fast exits)
- hardened overhead target: <200ns/call (bounded heavy path)

### What Counts As ŌĆ£Hot PathŌĆØ
- `RuntimeMathKernel::decide` on the strict path
- per-call evidence recording (if enabled)
- pointer-validation stage ordering / cached biases

### Hard Rules
- No transcendental math, matrix solves, or heap allocation on strict fast path.
- Any stochastic element must be deterministic from seed (replayable).
- Any ŌĆ£expensiveŌĆØ kernel must be cadence-driven (epoch update) not per call.

### Measurement Artifacts Required
- Criterion microbench for `decide` / `observe` (ns/op + percentiles)
- Profile recipe (flamegraph/perf) pinned to a reproducible command
- Golden snapshot outputs + sha256 regression gate
- Isomorphism proof template for policy changes (behavior invariants)

### Definition Of Done
- We can quantify overhead deltas per kernel addition.
- We can detect regressions in CI before merging.
- We have an explicit narrative for every optimization: what changed, why safe, how measured.

comments.text: EPIC CLOSED by CobaltCompass. All 7 sub-beads complete: (1) bd-d5l microbench (BlueLake), (2) bd-vj3 per-kernel bench suite (BrightMoose, verified CobaltCompass), (3) bd-1om golden snapshots (CobaltForge), (4) bd-pt6 profiling recipe (BlueLake), (5) bd-3dv hard rule audit (CobaltCompass - new enforcement script + invariant test), (6) bd-242 regression thresholds (BlueLake), (7) bd-38w isomorphism proof template (BrightMoose). description:
FOUND + FIXED via differential harness vs glibc (commit 0bd439d2).

strtod("1e400", &end) returned +Infinity (correct) but failed to set errno=ERANGE (POSIX violation). Same for strtod("1e-400", &end) which returned 0.0 without ERANGE. Glibc sets ERANGE in both cases.

Reachability: any program that uses strtod to parse numeric input and relies on errno to distinguish overflow-Inf from literal "INF" (or underflow-0 from literal "0") would silently misclassify. Common in JSON/config parsers and scientific code.

Fix in crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs strtod(): after parsing, set errno=ERANGE if val.is_infinite() AND consumed prefix doesn't contain "inf" literal, OR val==0.0 AND consumed prefix contains any non-zero digit.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 0bd439d2; differential test diff_strtod_cases now passes including 1e400/1e-400 ERANGE cases.

title: PORT: 10 nis_* name helpers + clone stubs (nis_domain_of/leaf_of/name_of + _r variants + nis_dir_cmp + 3 clone stubs)
description: Continue NIS+ libnsl parity. Ship 10 nis_* name-handling helpers + clone stubs. (1) nis_domain_of(name) -> *mut c_char: pointer into a thread-local static returning the name with its first label stripped (e.g. "host.subdom.dom." -> "subdom.dom."). (2) nis_domain_of_r(name, *buf, buflen) -> *mut c_char: same into caller buffer; ERANGE on overflow. (3) nis_leaf_of(name) -> *mut c_char: pointer into a thread-local returning the first label only ("host.subdom.dom." -> "host"). (4) nis_leaf_of_r(name, *buf, buflen): same into caller buffer. (5) nis_name_of(name): returns the "name part" with the local domain stripped; since our nis_local_directory is empty, behave as pass-through. (6) nis_name_of_r(name, *buf, buflen): pass-through into caller buffer. (7) nis_dir_cmp(a, b) -> int: case-insensitive compare of two NIS+ directory names; return 0/1/-1/-2 (LOWER_NAME / SAME_NAME / HIGHER_NAME / NOT_SEQUENTIAL) per nis_compare_t. (8)/(9)/(10) nis_clone_directory / nis_clone_object / nis_clone_result: stubs returning NULL.
close_reason: shipped: 7 nis_* name helpers (domain_of/leaf_of/name_of + _r) with backslash-aware first-unescaped-dot finder + nis_dir_cmp comparator + 3 clone stubs + 9 abi tests

title: PORT: __nss_lookup_function + __nss_hosts_lookup2 + __nss_next2 (NSS-internal stubs)
description: Port the remaining NSS-internal stub symbols missing from our libc surface: __nss_lookup_function (resolve a per-service function pointer in the NSS module loader; we have no NSS modules so always returns NULL), __nss_hosts_lookup2 (newer "v2" hosts lookup variant with explicit address-family + extended errno; UNAVAIL stub), __nss_next2 (advance to next NSS service with fallback-name selector; UNAVAIL stub). Same fail-graceful pattern as the already-shipped __nss_passwd_lookup / __nss_group_lookup / __nss_hosts_lookup / __nss_next family in glibc_internal_abi. Tests verify each stub resolves and returns the expected NSS_STATUS_UNAVAIL (-1) or NULL pointer.
close_reason: shipped: __nss_lookup_function + __nss_hosts_lookup2 + __nss_next2 NSS-internal UNAVAIL stubs matching existing pattern + 3 abi tests, all green

title: FUZZ: statx extended-mask + flags structure-aware harness
description: crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_statx.rs. Fuzzes dirfd (AT_FDCWD/-1/INT_MAX), path (seed list + fuzz bytes), mask (u32), flags (AT_*). 32-byte guard bands on both sides of a 256-byte statx buffer, verified intact post-call. Dev-note: initial harness had a too-strict 'stx_mask subset of requested' invariant; Linux always fills STATX_BASIC_STATS regardless of request_mask, so the invariant was removed after first-run crash discovery. 5.8M runs clean at 96K exec/s.

Many implemented stdio functions lack coverage: fgetc/fputc (char I/O), fgets/fputs (string I/O), feof/ferror (stream state), fileno, clearerr, ungetc, rewind, fgetpos/fsetpos. Add differential testing for character and string I/O operations.

title: PORT: 16 NIS+ nis_* fail-safe libnsl stubs (error formatters + local queries + free no-ops)
description: Add 16 NIS+ (nis_*) fail-safe stubs to extend the libnsl parity work. NIS+ is even deader than NIS ŌĆö sun deprecated it in the late 1990s ŌĆö but binaries that link against it through libc must still resolve at link-edit. Provide the cheapest possible safe behavior. Error helpers (4): nis_perror writes "<label>: NIS+ error" to fd 2; nis_sperrno returns a static description string for the given nis_error code; nis_sperror returns a malloc-allocated "<label>: <msg>" string; nis_sperror_r writes that into a caller buffer. Logger (1): nis_lerror is a no-op (syslog target). Free helpers (7): nis_freeresult / nis_freenames / nis_free_object / nis_free_directory / nis_free_request / nis_freeservlist / nis_freetags are all no-ops because our query stubs never allocate any of those structures. Local queries (4): nis_local_directory / nis_local_host / nis_local_principal / nis_local_group all return a pointer to a static empty C string. Error codes use the standard NIS+ enum (NIS_NAMEUNREACHABLE = 5 etc). 16 functions total.
close_reason: shipped: 16 NIS+ nis_* stubs (4 local queries + 7 free no-ops + 4 error formatters + nis_lerror) + 10 abi tests covering empty-string returns + NULL/bogus-pointer no-op safety + sperror text matches + ERANGE/EINVAL paths

title: bd-3bg subtask: Minimal codec scope/exclusion ledger + compatibility intent
description:
Background:
- iconv scope must be explicit to avoid overpromising full iconvdata parity before deterministic generation pipeline exists.

Goal:
- Define minimal codec scope, exclusions, and compatibility intent for phase progression.

Deliverables:
1) Codec inclusion/exclusion ledger with rationale.
2) Compatibility goals per included codec family.
3) Support matrix mapping for supported/unsupported codecs.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Scope is explicit and user-facing claims are accurate.
- No ambiguous codec support status.

Verification & Logging:
- Unit tests for scope manifest parsing/validation.
- Structured logs for scope checks and drift detections.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria
Implementation must preserve all existing functionality and claims while adding deterministic verification coverage.

### Mandatory Verification Gate
- Comprehensive unit tests are required for all touched logic: positive, negative, adversarial, and regression.
- Deterministic e2e scripts are required for all externally visible behavior in strict and hardened mode where relevant.
- Structured logs are mandatory for unit/e2e runs with fields: `trace_id`, `mode`, `api_family`, `symbol`, `decision_path`, `healing_action`, `errno`, `latency_ns`, `artifact_refs`.
- Closure evidence must include exact test commands, expected outputs/thresholds, and representative failure signatures.
- CI must persist logs, diffs, and reports with deterministic naming and retention for replay/triage.
- Tooling contract: deterministic orchestration/traceability uses `/dp/asupersync`, and deterministic diff/snapshot/TUI evidence uses `/dp/frankentui`.
notes:
### Mandatory Test/E2E Logging Checklist
- Test command set is documented in bead notes before closure (unit + e2e + perf where applicable).
- Expected output and pass/fail thresholds are documented per command.
- Failure triage snippets are documented (top failure classes + where to inspect artifacts).
- Evidence index entry links logs, parity diffs, and benchmark/proof artifacts.
- Overload behavior logging is required when applicable: pressure signal, degradation mode, selected policy, guard decision, rollback trigger.
close_reason: Implemented deterministic iconv codec scope ledger drift gate, structured evidence logs, and CI integration

title: PORT: pidfile_open + pidfile_write + pidfile_close + pidfile_remove + pidfile_fileno (FreeBSD libutil pidfile)
description: FreeBSD/libbsd pidfile(3) family ŌĆö pidfile_open(path, mode, *otherpid) opens or creates a daemon PID file with an exclusive flock; if another process already holds the lock it returns NULL with errno=EEXIST and writes the locker pid to *otherpid (parsed from the file contents). pidfile_write writes the current pid as ASCII into the file. pidfile_close releases the lock without unlinking (for child-takeover patterns). pidfile_remove unlinks and closes (standard cleanup). pidfile_fileno returns the underlying fd. Used by sshd, mysqld, postgresql, ntpd, and basically every BSD/Linux daemon. Currently missing entirely from frankenlibc-abi. Ship: thin shims in stdlib_abi.rs over the just-landed flopen + pwrite + ftruncate + read + unlink, with a malloc"d struct pidfh storing fd + path so pidfile_remove can unlink without re-resolving the path.
close_reason: shipped: stdlib_abi pidfile_open/write/close/remove/fileno over flopen + opaque PidFh struct; 9 tests; clippy-clean; rch-built

title: PORT: strtonum (OpenBSD bounded decimal parser)
description: OpenBSD strtonum(nptr, minval, maxval, errstr) parses nptr as a decimal long long integer constrained to [minval, maxval] and returns the value plus an opaque error category through *errstr (NULL on success, "invalid"/"too small"/"too large"/"out of range" on failure). Used by OpenBSD utilities (sshd, pf, bgpd) for safer numeric parsing than strtoll(3) ŌĆö no errno fiddling, no overflow gotchas, range check baked in. Currently missing from frankenlibc-abi/stdlib_abi.rs. Ship: pure-safe core::stdlib::strtonum::parse returning Result<i64, StrtonumError> with Invalid/TooSmall/TooLarge/InvalidRange variants + thin abi shim that maps the error to OpenBSD-canonical static error strings.
close_reason: shipped: core::stdlib::strtonum + stdlib_abi::strtonum; 25+12 tests; clippy-clean; rch-built

title: PORT: vis + nvis (NetBSD vis(3) single-byte encoders)
description: NetBSD vis(3) single-byte encoders: vis(dst, c, flags, nextc) writes the visual encoding of byte c into dst (NUL terminated), returning a pointer to the trailing NUL. nvis(dst, dlen, c, flags, nextc) is the bounded variant returning NULL on overflow. The nextc parameter disambiguates \\NNN sequences when octal output could otherwise blur into a following digit; with our 3-digit-padded octal it has no effect, but we accept and document it. Companion to the strvis/strnvis/strunvis/strnunvis quartet shipped in bd-i18zl. Currently missing entirely from frankenlibc-abi. Ship: thin shims in stdio_abi.rs over the existing core::stdio::vis::encode_byte primitive.

Add crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_strftime.rs that exercises frankenlibc_core::time::format_strftime with arbitrary format bytes + arbitrary BrokenDownTime fields and a fixed output buffer. Differential check vs libc::strftime is hard (locale state matters); start with crash-detector + invariant checks: no panic, no buffer overrun (return value <= maxsize), determinism on repeated calls. Per /testing-fuzzing skill (Archetype 1).

title: P0: gcvt writes past tracked small output buffer
description:
[CRITICAL] The gcvt ABI wrapper constructs a mutable slice from the caller's raw buffer using ndigit + 32 bytes, capped at 512, without consulting malloc_abi::known_remaining(buf). A tracked caller allocation smaller than that assumed size can therefore be turned into an overlong &mut [u8] and written past before the core formatter truncates to that artificial slice size.

Evidence:
- crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs:3218 accepts the raw caller buf pointer.
- crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs:3226-3228 assumes buf_size = ndigit.max(0) + 32 (min 512 cap) and calls from_raw_parts_mut(buf, buf_size).
- crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs:3229 passes that slice to frankenlibc_core::stdlib::gcvt, which writes rendered bytes plus a NUL up to the slice length.
- The module already imports known_remaining and uses it broadly for other stdlib pointer surfaces, but gcvt does not cap the writable slice by the tracked allocation.

Repro shape: p = malloc(1); call gcvt(123.456, 10, p). Expected hardened/TSM behavior is to cap to known_remaining(p) or reject safely. Actual code creates a 42-byte mutable slice from a 1-byte tracked allocation and may write beyond it.

Fix direction: compute tracked = known_remaining(buf as usize); choose a writable length bounded by tracked when present, and return buf after writing only within that length. Add an ABI regression using malloc-backed 1-byte and 2-byte buffers so the wrapper preserves the canary/neighbor bytes while still NUL-terminating where possible.
close_reason: fixed: gcvt caps malloc-tracked output buffers with known_remaining; validation: stdlib_abi_test gcvt, diff_gcvt_cases, cargo check/clippy -p frankenlibc-abi, UBS

close_reason: done

title: syscall: eventfd_write invalid fd should preserve EBADF even for forbidden all-ones payload
description: Host glibc returns EBADF for eventfd_write(-1, UINT64_MAX), so the invalid-fd contract dominates the forbidden payload. Add ABI regression coverage for that precedence.
close_reason: done

title: setjmp_abi tests::longjmp_entrypoint_terminates_with_enosys_payload_in_tests flakes under high parallelism
description:
## Repro

```bash
for i in {1..8}; do
  CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenlibc_cc cargo test \
    -p frankenlibc-abi --tests -- --test-threads=16 \
    | grep -E "FAILED|invalid size|signal:"
done
```

After ~3-4 iterations the unit test
`setjmp_abi::tests::longjmp_entrypoint_terminates_with_enosys_payload_in_tests`
asserts FAILED. Running with `--test-threads=1` does not reproduce.
Found while stress-testing the bd-wbqeo per-thread reentry refactor;
the failure is unrelated to that change (also reproduces against
prior commits ŌĆö file history shows the test pattern is unchanged).

## Suspected cause

The setjmp/longjmp test uses a shared
`OnceLock<Mutex<HashMap<usize, JumpRegistryEntry>>>` keyed by env
pointer address (crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/setjmp_abi.rs:44). The
unit test creates a stack-local `marker: [u64; 16]` and uses its
address as the env_addr key. Two test functions running on different
worker threads get distinct stack addresses, but a single worker
thread that runs tests sequentially reuses the same stack frame
addresses across tests ŌĆö the registry entry from a prior test
remains for that key and is overwritten by the next capture. The
new entry's bytes should match the new capture's mem_bytes, but
under contention or specific scheduling the read-back of mem_bytes
in restore_env compares against a stale or partially-updated entry,
returning EFAULT (22) instead of triggering the deferred
ENOSYS-panic path the test asserts.

The test then panics with errno=22 reason=`invalid_or_foreign_jump_context`
instead of errno=38 reason=`deferred_longjmp_transfer_backend`.

## Fix candidates

1. Test-only: each test should remove its registry entry on exit
   (RAII guard that calls `registry().lock().remove(&env_addr)`
   when the test scope ends).
2. Production: registry could key on (thread_id, env_addr) instead
   of just env_addr ŌĆö this would handle both intentional cross-
   thread longjmp (illegal per POSIX, EINVAL is correct) and the
   stack-frame-reuse case across sequential tests on the same worker.
3. Test-only: serialize the setjmp tests with a #[serial] attribute
   or a shared TEST_LOCK Mutex so they don't race for stack address
   reuse.

## Severity

[MEDIUM] (P3) ŌĆö test-only flake, doesn't affect production behavior.
But it does mask real failures by polluting CI noise. Suggest fix #1
(simplest) or #3 (most robust) as the swarm has time.
notes: Fixed in 6ee22896: RegistryEntryGuard RAII wrapper added to all six setjmp_abi unit tests. Validated by 12x stress with --test-threads=16: zero longjmp_entrypoint_terminates_with_enosys_payload_in_tests failures. Three OTHER unrelated flakes surfaced in same sweep ŌĆö filed separately.

title: PORT: fgetwln (NetBSD libutil wide-char line reader)
description: NetBSD libutil fgetwln(stream, *lenp): wide-char counterpart of fgetln. Read a line of wchar_t from stream up to and including the trailing newline, return pointer into a thread-local wchar_t buffer plus length (in wchars) via *lenp. Returns NULL on EOF/error with *lenp=0. Buffer invalidated by next call. Mirror existing fgetln impl over fgetc; here loop over fgetwc and accumulate Vec<libc::wchar_t> in a thread_local RefCell.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] readdir holds DIR_REGISTRY mutex across sys_getdents64 ŌĆö cross-dir contention on slow FS
description: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/dirent_abi.rs:153 readdir acquires DIR_REGISTRY.lock() and holds it through state.get_mut() + sys_getdents64 at line 192. sys_getdents64 is a blocking syscall (can take seconds on NFS/FUSE). While it blocks, every other thread's opendir/readdir/closedir on ANY directory blocks on the mutex. Classic Class 1 anti-pattern: blocking call while holding lock. Fix (larger scope): replace the global Mutex<Option<HashMap>> with per-DirState mutexes or a sharded map; OR swap-remove state under the lock, call sys_getdents64 without the lock, then re-insert. Not a correctness bug (single-dir readdir works fine) but a scalability defect that manifests as unexplained multi-second stalls when one dir operation is slow.
close_reason: Replaced global DIR registry state with per-directory Arc<Mutex<DirState>> handles so getdents64 does not hold the registry lock; added concurrent independent readdir regression.

title: [MODE-1][HIGH] symbolic correctness: c11threads create/join backend mismatch
description:
[MODE-1][HIGH] symbolic correctness: C11 thread create/join backend contract is internally inconsistent

Validated by current remote gate failure:
- `env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenlibc_cod rch exec -- cargo test --workspace --all-targets`
- failing suite: `crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/c11threads_abi_test.rs`

Concrete mismatch:
- `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/c11threads_abi.rs:72-110` calls `crate::pthread_abi::pthread_create`, which prefers host `pthread_create` whenever `resolved_thread_create_raw()` is available (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs:1991-2015`).
- `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/c11threads_abi.rs:118-124` then calls `crate::pthread_abi::pthread_join`.
- but `pthread_join` is hard-routed to `native_pthread_join` (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs:2019-2021`), which only accepts handles present in `THREAD_HANDLE_REGISTRY` and returns `ESRCH` otherwise (`crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs:1409-1445`).

For host-created threads, the symbolic contract `join(create(f, arg)) = THRD_SUCCESS + propagated return value` is broken: the handle identity model used by `create` is not the one consumed by `join`.

Observed fallout already matches that model break:
- `test_thrd_create_join`
- `test_thrd_join_propagates_zero`
- `test_thrd_join_propagates_negative`
- `test_thrd_join_propagates_large`

Fix direction:
- make C11 thread lifecycle choose one backend consistently across create/join/detach/self/equal, or
- teach `pthread_join` to resolve host-thread handles through the host path instead of assuming every successful create registered a native handle.
close_reason: Duplicate of bd-21ypi / bd-jqy64 ŌĆö same c11threads create/join backend mismatch. Fix landed in commit 138c07c3 (pthread_join now delegates to host for non-managed handles). c11threads_abi_test suite is green (36/0). bd-jqy64 remains in_progress under cod's ownership.

title: [MODE-3][MEDIUM] ABI compatibility: printf executor hardcodes LP64 widths
description:
[MODE-3][MEDIUM] ABI compatibility: printf conformance executor hardcodes LP64 vararg widths

Recent printf executor work widened promotion and dispatch handling, but the ABI model is still host-width-specific:
- `crates/frankenlibc_conformance/src/lib.rs:2492-2496` stores long-like integers as `PrintfArg::Long(c_longlong)` and `PrintfArg::Ulong(c_ulonglong)`.
- `crates/frankenlibc_conformance/src/lib.rs:2502-2585` treats `%l`, `%j`, `%z`, `%t`, and `%q` as a single `long_like` bucket and rewrites integer args into those 64-bit slots.

That matches the current LP64 host, but it is not an ABI-stable model for width-sensitive formats such as `%ld`, `%zd`, and `%td`. On non-LP64 targets the executor can mis-marshal arguments and report false conformance parity.

This is an ABI review finding rather than a current Linux x86_64 failure, but it directly affects whether the conformance harness can be trusted when the matrix expands past today's host ABI.

Fix direction:
- split `long`, `long long`, `size_t`, `ptrdiff_t`, and `intmax_t` by actual target width
- source widths from explicit target metadata instead of one 64-bit promotion bucket.
notes:
Closed by cc-libc as duplicate of bd-1tvbi (already closed at commit b624a988 on 2026-04-22T09:32Z).

Identical description: same file (crates/frankenlibc_conformance/src/lib.rs), same line ranges (2492-2585), same set of LP64-width concerns (%l, %j, %z, %t, %q bucketing into a single c_longlong slot), same fix direction (split-by-ABI-width refactor gated on target metadata).

The accepted fix in bd-1tvbi was pragmatic: make the LP64 assumption explicit via three compile-time size_of asserts (see crates/frankenlibc_conformance/src/lib.rs:2520-2532) that fail the build on any target where c_long / size_t / ptrdiff_t is narrower than c_longlong. The three assertion messages all point back to 'refactor bd-1tvbi before building for non-LP64 targets', which bound the scope correctly: the executor stays correct on every LP64 target we currently support, and the moment a non-LP64 target is added to the matrix the build fails loudly with a direct pointer at the bead.

Re-opening bd-8sgm6 as its own refactor scope would duplicate that work. When the ABI matrix actually expands, reopen bd-1tvbi (not this bead) to do the full split-by-width refactor.

title: PORT: set_robust_list / get_robust_list + lsm_get_self_attr / set_self_attr / list_modules
description: Add 5 more Linux libc syscall wrappers. (1) set_robust_list(*head, len) -> int (SYS_set_robust_list=273) ŌĆö register a per-thread robust futex list head so the kernel can clean up if the thread exits while holding mutexes. (2) get_robust_list(pid, **head_ptr, *len_ptr) -> int (SYS_get_robust_list=274) ŌĆö read the registered head + len for the given pid (or 0 = self). (3) lsm_get_self_attr(attr_id, *ctx, *size, flags) -> int (Linux 6.8+, syscall 459) ŌĆö read an LSM (SELinux/AppArmor/Smack/...) attribute for the calling thread into a buffer, with *size updated. (4) lsm_set_self_attr(attr_id, *ctx, size, flags) -> int (Linux 6.8+, syscall 460) ŌĆö write an LSM attribute for the calling thread. (5) lsm_list_modules(*ids, *size, flags) -> int (Linux 6.8+, syscall 461) ŌĆö enumerate LSM modules active in the running kernel. All forward via libc::syscall + raw_syscall_with_errno bridge; NULL-pointer guards return EFAULT where the kernel would otherwise UB.
close_reason: shipped: 5 syscall wrappers (set_robust_list, get_robust_list, lsm_get_self_attr, lsm_set_self_attr, lsm_list_modules) + 8 abi tests with thread-runner robust-list round-trip preserved via save/restore

title: search: bound hsearch key scans

A tracked key without a NUL can therefore be read past its allocation before hsearch rejects it. Use known_remaining for bounded key scans and add global hsearch regression coverage beside the existing hsearch_r case.

title: PORT: __cxa_call_unexpected + __cxa_get_globals(_fast) (Itanium C++ ABI runtime stubs)
description: Port the remaining Itanium C++ ABI runtime entry points missing from our libc surface: __cxa_call_unexpected(exception_obj) ŌĆö invoked when a thrown exception does not match the dynamic exception specification of the function it would propagate through; per ABI it calls std::unexpected which by default calls std::terminate. For our runtime (no real exception support) we mirror __cxa_pure_virtual / __cxa_throw_bad_array_new_length: write a diagnostic to stderr and abort. __cxa_get_globals() and __cxa_get_globals_fast() ŌĆö return a thread-local pointer to a __cxa_eh_globals struct (caughtExceptions list head + uncaughtExceptions counter) used by the exception ABI to track in-flight exceptions; we expose a per-thread zero-initialized pair so callers that only inspect the counter (e.g. std::uncaught_exceptions()) get a coherent answer. Implementation: __cxa_call_unexpected is a no-return abort path; __cxa_get_globals/_fast return a thread-local AtomicPtr-backed stable address. Tests: fork+SIGABRT for __cxa_call_unexpected, address-stability test for the get_globals pair.
close_reason: shipped: __cxa_call_unexpected fail-stop + __cxa_get_globals/_fast with per-thread CxaEhGlobals + 3 abi tests, all green

title: REVIEW: [HIGH] __assert_perror_fail walks unbounded memory via CStr::from_ptr, inconsistent with __assert_fail hardening
description:
Location: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs:3248-3257 (__assert_perror_fail).

__assert_fail (line 3197) carefully uses scan_c_string(ptr, known_remaining(ptr as usize)) with a defined-length bound on every string pointer (assertion, file, function) and substitutes '??' when the bound can't be satisfied. __assert_perror_fail does the opposite:
    let f = if file.is_null() { c"??" } else { unsafe { CStr::from_ptr(file) } };
    let func = if function.is_null() { c"??" } else { unsafe { CStr::from_ptr(function) } };
CStr::from_ptr walks bytes until it encounters a NUL ŌĆö with no bound. An adversarial or corrupted caller passing a non-NUL-terminated pointer walks unbounded process memory. Consequences:
- Information leak: arbitrary process memory bytes flushed through the error message to stderr.
- Segfault: if the walk crosses an unmapped-page boundary.

Reachability: exported libc surface. Any assert_perror() call made from untrusted/foreign code, or any plain call to __assert_perror_fail with crafted pointers, triggers this. In practice __FILE__/__FUNCTION__ are always NUL-terminated string literals, but the libc boundary does not enforce that ŌĆö the compiler guarantee does not cover hostile callers.

Severity: HIGH (info leak / segfault reachable from a single libc entrypoint), though mitigated in practice by macro-expanded call sites that happen to use string literals.

Fix plan:
- Mirror __assert_fail's hardening: scan_c_string with known_remaining(ptr as usize), substitute '??' when the string is null OR not terminated within the known-remaining region.
- Add a regression test using a non-null-terminated buffer ŌĆö prior to the fix this would segfault or leak neighboring bytes; after the fix it must substitute '??' cleanly.
close_reason: Fixed: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs __assert_perror_fail now mirrors __assert_fail's hardening ŌĆö both file and function pointers are read via scan_c_string(ptr, known_remaining(ptr as usize)) and substituted with '??' when null or not terminated within the known region. Prior behavior used CStr::from_ptr which walks memory until an accidental NUL is hit, letting an adversarial caller either leak bytes into the stderr message or crash on an unmapped page. Abort-path entrypoints cannot be tested in-process (per the existing fortify_abi_test.rs convention: 'The abort paths (__chk_fail) cannot be tested in-process since they call abort()'). Verified by rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_libc_review cargo check -p frankenlibc-abi --all-targets -> clean in 3.36s. The fix re-uses the already-tested read-bounded pattern from __assert_fail at lines 3198-3232.
