title: stdio: export _IO_2_1_stdin_/stdout_/stderr_ aliases bound to NativeFile storage
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦3 (final paragraph). Some statically-linked-against-libc binaries resolve _IO_2_1_stdin_, _IO_2_1_stdout_, _IO_2_1_stderr_ directly via dlsym or link-time resolution, bypassing the public stdin/stdout/stderr aliases. Without exporting these names from FrankenLibC, the binary sees two distinct streams (one native FrankenLibC, one host glibc) and the program misbehaves subtly: partial flushes, lost output, double-close.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile layout)

## What to do

1. Add the three symbol names to crates/frankenlibc-abi/version_scripts/libc.map under the GLIBC_2.2.5 version tag.
2. In stdio_abi.rs (or io_internal_abi.rs), declare the underscored names as #[no_mangle] static aliases pointing to the *same* NativeFile storage as the public stdin/stdout/stderr globals. Do NOT allocate a second set of NativeFile objects ŌĆö they must be the same object so a flush via stdin reaches the same buffer as a flush via _IO_2_1_stdin_.
3. Verify the alias by building the .so and inspecting nm: nm -D target/release/libfrankenlibc_abi.so | grep _IO_2_1 must show the three symbols at the same address as stdin/stdout/stderr.
4. Add an integration test: build a small C program that takes both stdin and _IO_2_1_stdin_, asserts they are equal, and runs it under LD_PRELOAD.

## Done

- libc.map exports the three aliases
- nm verification passes
- Integration test passes
- The aliases survive a release build with strip+lto
close_reason: done

title: stdio: build NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T vtable + 17 trampolines for indirect dispatch
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦10. Many glibc-built binaries (especially with -fno-plt) call vfprintf indirectly through ((struct _IO_FILE_plus *)fp)->vtable->__xsputn(...) rather than through the PLT-resolved vfprintf symbol. If our vtable pointer is null or wrong, the program crashes inside __xsputn.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile must already have a vtable slot)

## What to do

1. In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/io_internal_abi.rs, define repr(C) struct _IO_jump_t with the 17 standard slots: __dummy, __dummy2, __finish, __overflow, __underflow, __uflow, __pbackfail, __xsputn, __xsgetn, __seekoff, __seekpos, __setbuf, __sync, __doallocate, __read, __write, __seek, __close, __stat, __showmanyc, __imbue. Field offsets pinned to glibc 2.34.
2. Create a static NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T: _IO_jump_t = ... initialized with extern "C" function pointers to Rust trampolines.
3. Each trampoline takes a *mut FILE, casts to *mut NativeFile, acquires the recursive lock, and dispatches to the native kernel function used by the public ABI entrypoint. The trampolines for __xsputn and __xsgetn are the high-volume ones; benchmark them.
4. Every NativeFile constructor (bd-9chy.5..10) sets `self.vtable = &NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T as *const _ as *mut _`.
5. Trampoline must handle the foreign-pointer-adoption case: if the FILE* it receives has the bloom precheck saying "not ours," route through adopt_foreign_file (separate bead bd-9chy.16). For now, panic with a clear message; the next bead wires real adoption.
6. Unit test: build a small Rust test that takes &mut NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T.__xsputn, calls it with a NativeFile* and verifies bytes are written.

## Done

- _IO_jump_t struct defined with offsets matching glibc
- NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T static built and wired into NativeFile constructors
- All 17 trampolines have implementations (some can be no-ops or default behavior)
- Unit test for indirect __xsputn dispatch passes
- Snapshot test tests/runtime_math/golden/io_jump_dispatch.v1.json captures vtable-vs-PLT call counts on a representative workload
close_reason: Implemented _IO_jump_t vtable with 21 trampolines for indirect dispatch. All slots wired to native stdio functions. NativeFile::new() sets vtable to NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T. Layout tests added. Also fixed TLS corruption and type mismatches.

title: stdio: implement adopt_foreign_file via membrane bloom precheck + fd extraction
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦11. When a dlopend library opens a file BEFORE LD_PRELOAD takes effect and then hands the FILE* back to the LD_PRELOAD-ed binary, the pointer is host glibcs, not ours. Today the code "handles" this by delegating to host stdio (which is exactly the problem the epic exists to fix). Option B requires we *adopt* such pointers into the native registry instead.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.15 (vtable trampolines need a place to call the adoption path)

## What to do

1. In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdio_abi.rs (or io_internal_abi.rs), add `pub(crate) fn adopt_foreign_file(fp: *mut FILE) -> *mut NativeFile`.
2. Step 1 of every public stdio entrypoint AND every vtable trampoline: run the membrane bloom-filter ownership precheck (existing primitive in crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/bloom.rs). Cost: ~10 ns. Well within strict-mode budget.
3. If precheck says "ours," dispatch to the kernel directly.
4. If precheck says "not ours," call adopt_foreign_file:
   a. Read ((_IO_FILE *)fp)->_fileno to recover the underlying fd. (We do NOT read other fields ŌĆö they may be in a different layout from glibcs.) The _fileno offset is fixed at offset 0x70 in glibc 2.34 for x86_64; pin this constant in code with a comment explaining why we deliberately read it raw.
   b. Allocate a NativeFile shell, set its fd to the recovered value, set buffering mode based on isatty(fileno) (raw syscall), set orientation = Undecided, vtable = &NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T.
   c. Insert into stream registry indexed by BOTH the original foreign pointer AND the new native pointer (so subsequent calls with the foreign pointer find the same NativeFile).
   d. Emit a FOREIGN_FILE_ADOPTED evidence record (uses the schema from bd-9chy.4).
   e. Return the new NativeFile *.
5. Generation safety: if the same fd is later closed and reopened, the new NativeFile gets a fresh generation; the old foreign-pointer registry entry is invalidated. Subsequent stdio calls against the stale foreign pointer return EBADF and emit FOREIGN_FILE_STALE.
6. Test: build a test C program that calls dlopen("libc.so.6") and uses dlsym to get fopen, opens a file via that dlsym-resolved fopen, and passes the FILE* to FrankenLibCs fread. Verify the adoption happens (assert via the evidence ring buffer).

## Done

- adopt_foreign_file implemented
- Every stdio entrypoint and trampoline runs the bloom precheck first
- Adoption test passes
- Evidence records appear in the ring buffer for the test
- Stale-pointer EBADF path covered by a separate unit test
close_reason: done

title: stdio: per-FILE parking_lot::ReentrantMutex + flockfile/funlockfile/ftrylockfile + *_unlocked
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦1.11. POSIX requires every standard stdio function to be thread-safe via implicit per-FILE locking, with flockfile/funlockfile/ftrylockfile for explicit batching, and the *_unlocked family for callers that already hold the lock. v1 ignored this entirely.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile must already have the lock slot)

## What to do

1. NativeFileState contains parking_lot::ReentrantMutex<NativeFileLocked>. The locked region wraps buffer cursors, eof/error flags, ungetc, mbstate, orientation, healing budget.
2. Every public ABI fn (fread, fwrite, ...) acquires the lock via reentrant_mutex.lock() before entering the kernel; releases on exit (RAII via guard).
3. Every *_unlocked variant (fread_unlocked, fwrite_unlocked, fgetc_unlocked, fputc_unlocked, fgets_unlocked, fputs_unlocked, feof_unlocked, ferror_unlocked, clearerr_unlocked, fileno_unlocked) skips the acquisition AND under #[cfg(debug_assertions)] asserts the caller already holds the lock (parking_lots ReentrantMutex exposes is_locked / try_lock for this).
4. flockfile / funlockfile / ftrylockfile (verify they exist in stdio_abi.rs; if not, add them) operate on the same recursive mutex.
5. The recursive lock count must survive setjmp/longjmp correctly (deferred to bd-9chy.18, the longjmp lock-stack bead).
6. Add an integration test that fans out 32 threads, each writing 1024 4-byte records to a shared FILE*; reads back; verifies record-level interleaving (no torn writes). Both strict and hardened modes.

## Done

- Every public ABI fn takes the lock; every *_unlocked fn does not
- ReentrantMutex used (not Mutex ŌĆö POSIX requires recursive)
- 32-thread stress test passes in both modes
- flockfile/funlockfile/ftrylockfile work correctly under nested lock
notes: Implemented flockfile/funlockfile/ftrylockfile. NativeFile already has ReentrantMutex in NativeFileState. Remaining: verify all ABI fns use locking, audit *_unlocked variants, add stress test.
close_reason: done

title: support_matrix: tighten classifier to inspect ABI function bodies and add WrapsHostLibc state
description:
## Background

Reality-check found that tests/conformance/support_matrix_maintenance_report.v1.json was regenerated 2026-04-08 with zero deltas vs the 2026-02-18 baseline, despite ongoing March/April commits to ABI files including stdio. The current classifier in scripts/generate_support_matrix_maintenance.py (and the Rust harness equivalent in crates/frankenlibc-harness if it exists) inspects only signatures, not bodies. As a result, ABI functions that are labelled Implemented but in fact call libc::fwrite or host_*_fn() at runtime are not flagged.

This bead is the body-inspecting classifier and the new explicit WrapsHostLibc taxonomy state. It runs *first* in the epic order because every other bead depends on having a mechanical way to verify "did I close the gap I think I closed."

## What to do

1. Add a new variant WrapsHostLibc to the support taxonomy (alongside Implemented, RawSyscall, GlibcCallThrough, Stub). Update the JSON schema, the Rust enum if any, and the README/AGENTS.md taxonomy descriptions.
2. Extend scripts/generate_support_matrix_maintenance.py to walk every *_abi.rs file in crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/ via syn (or tree-sitter, or naive ripgrep ŌĆö the rule is structural).
3. For each public extern "C" fn, check the body for: any libc::f* call (fopen/fclose/fread/fwrite/fseek/ftell/fflush/setvbuf/ungetc/feof/ferror/clearerr/fileno/getline/getdelim/printf), any libc::malloc/free/realloc, any host_*_fn() invocation, any crate::host_resolve::resolve_host_symbol_* call. If any match, classify as WrapsHostLibc.
4. Regenerate support_matrix.json. EXPECT a one-time bump: stdio symbols + rpc/glibc_internal symbols flip from Implemented to WrapsHostLibc. This is the corrective action ŌĆö the count change is the audit telling the truth for the first time.
5. Add the L0 closure gate clause in tests/conformance/closure_contract.v1.json: WrapsHostLibc must be 0 for L0 closure.
6. Run tests/conformance/check_support_matrix_maintenance.sh and confirm it now produces a non-trivial delta vs the prior baseline.

## Done

- New WrapsHostLibc state exists in schema and code
- Classifier inspects function bodies, not signatures
- support_matrix.json regenerates with the corrective bump
- closure_contract.v1.json gates on WrapsHostLibc == 0
- CI fails on a freshly added libc::fwrite in any *_abi.rs file (verify with a one-line test PR)
- README and AGENTS.md taxonomy descriptions updated to mention WrapsHostLibc
close_reason: Completed body-aware WrapsHostLibc classifier, maintenance gate, and closure-contract integration

title: stdio: build offline synthesis pipeline tools/stdio_synth/ for printf/scanf grammars + table compiler + SMT prover
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦1.4 + ┬¦1.8 + ┬¦16. Stdio is reverse-round anchor #5 in AGENTS.md ("eliminate parser-state explosions and locale drift; ship generated parser/transducer tables"). The Required Methodology says: spec ŌåÆ synthesis ŌåÆ compact runtime artifact + proof. The current hand-written crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdio/printf.rs (1446 LOC) and scanf.rs (1026 LOC) are NOT what the methodology calls for.

This bead replaces them with a build-time synthesis pipeline that emits proven tables.

## What to do

1. Create tools/stdio_synth/ (NEW directory, lives OUTSIDE the libc workspace ŌĆö does not pollute the .so):
   - spec/printf_grammar.json: formal grammar of POSIX printf format language as a typed regular tree (flag set ├Ś width ├Ś precision ├Ś length ├Ś conversion).
   - spec/scanf_grammar.json: same for scanf.
   - synth/printf_compiler.rs: Rust binary; consumes the grammar; emits a const dispatch table PRINTF_TABLE: [PrintfRoute; 256] into crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdio/printf_tables.rs.
   - synth/proof.rs: for each generated route, emits an SMT-LIB script asserting that the routes behavior matches a small reference interpreter encoded in the grammar. Run via cvc5 (or z3) at build time. Build fails on any unproven obligation.
   - synth/coverage.json: generated covering-array confirming every (flag ├Ś length ├Ś conversion) interaction is exercised. AGENTS.md obligation #17 (covering-array conformance interaction coverage).
   - synth/symmetry.rs: define the symmetry group acting on the (route ├Ś invariant) lattice (AGENTS.md obligation #35 Atiyah-Bott localization). Reuses the existing crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/runtime_math/atiyah_bott.rs primitive.
   - synth/compress.rs: consumes the symmetry group and the obligation set, emits a quotiented DAG. SMT obligations discharged on the quotient (~120 obligations instead of ~30k = 250x reduction).
2. Hand-written crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdio/printf.rs becomes a 200-line dispatcher consuming the generated table, plus float-printing helpers (Grisu/Ryu ŌĆö not table-friendly).
3. Same architecture for scanf.
4. Build-time budget: printf+scanf SMT phase Ōēż 90 s on a single core. Gated by tools/stdio_synth/check_smt_budget.sh.
5. Wire into Cargo.toml build.rs.

## Why this is its own bead and why it is high-priority

Without this, the project is "porting glibc by hand," which AGENTS.md "Clean-Room Porting" rule #2 explicitly forbids. With it, the Atiyah-Bott controller moves from "telemetry-only" to "actively used at build time," which is a small-but-real win for the runtime_math honesty story.

## Done

- tools/stdio_synth/ exists with grammar, compiler, prover, symmetry, compress, coverage
- printf.rs and scanf.rs reduced to <300 LOC dispatchers + table consumers
- printf_tables.rs and scanf_tables.rs are auto-generated and checked-in
- cargo build invokes the synthesis and the prover; build fails on any unproven obligation
- check_smt_budget.sh keeps SMT cost Ōēż 90 s
- The generated tables byte-equal across runs (deterministic)
close_reason: Already delivered; re-closing after JSONL drift reset status

title: stdio: encode 11 invariants in tests/conformance/stdio_invariants.v1.json + harness verifier
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦1.5 enumerates the 11 per-entrypoint invariants for stdio (fopen, fread, fwrite, fseek, ftell, fclose, ungetc, fflush, fileno, feof/ferror, clearerr) as proof obligations on the native kernel. Currently they live in prose inside the plan doc; this bead encodes them as a machine-checkable contract.

## What to do

1. Create tests/conformance/stdio_invariants.v1.json with one entry per entrypoint. Each entry has: function name, pre-conditions (boolean expressions over input parameters and the registry state), post-conditions (boolean expressions over return value, errno, the registry, and observable side effects), and the lattice transition (which SafetyState the call may move the FILE through).
2. Define a tiny invariant DSL that can express "fp is in registry," "fp.fd >= 0," "return <= n," "fp.eof set if return < n," etc. JSON form is fine; no need for a real parser.
3. Extend crates/frankenlibc-harness/src/verify.rs (or add a new module verify_invariants.rs) to load the invariants file and check each invariant against every fixture run from tests/conformance/fixtures/stdio/. The verifier emits a structured pass/fail per (fixture, invariant) cell.
4. Wire into scripts/check_conformance_fixture_pipeline.sh ŌĆö failures block the gate.
5. The 11 invariants are listed verbatim in plan v4 ┬¦1.5 and must be encoded faithfully. Phase 5 refinement may add more (e.g. for popen, tmpfile, fmemopen, fopencookie, getline) once those entrypoints have native implementations.

## Why this matters

Without machine-checkable invariants, "fread post-condition" lives in someones head. With this bead, every fixture run is a witness that the invariants held. The lattice transition column also feeds into the runtime_math cohomology controller (which monitors cross-shard consistency on stdio state), tightening ┬¦12 of the plan.

## Done

- stdio_invariants.v1.json exists with 11 entries
- Verifier loads and checks them
- Existing fixture runs report no invariant violations
- A deliberately-broken test fixture (e.g. an fread that returns more than n) is correctly flagged

title: stdio: define versioned JSONL evidence schema + parser + ring buffer
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦14. Every healing event, every foreign-pointer adoption, every POMDP repair decision, every membrane stage attribution emits a JSONL row to target/conformance/stdio_evidence.jsonl. The schema is versioned and a parser lives in crates/frankenlibc-harness/src/stdio_evidence.rs.

## What to do

1. Define the schema (schema_version=1) per plan v4 ┬¦14: timestamp_unix_ns, process (pid/tid/comm), mode, event_kind enum, function name, fp_hex, fp_origin enum, fd, params object, result object (return/errno/elapsed_ns), membrane_stages object (per-stage ns), runtime_math object (pomdp_action / conformal_band / cohomology_consistent / cvar_alarm), healing_action option, evidence_ring_seq monotonic counter, trace_id uuid.
2. Add a thread-local trace_id generator in crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/evidence.rs (which already exists per the audit at runtime_math/evidence.rs ŌĆö extend rather than create).
3. Write the JSONL serializer (use serde_json with a stable field order). Atomic ring buffer for backpressure: drop oldest on overflow with a counter increment so consumers see the drop.
4. Add the parser in crates/frankenlibc-harness/src/stdio_evidence.rs with a public Iterator<Item=StdioEvidenceRow> that consumes the JSONL file.
5. Test: cargo test -p frankenlibc-harness --test stdio_evidence_schema verifies round-trip serialize/deserialize, schema_version handling, trace_id propagation, ring buffer overflow counter.
6. Document compat policy in the parser doc-comment: backwards-compat shim required for at least the previous schema version (this is one of the rare exceptions to AGENTS.md "no backwards compat" rule, because evidence is an output artifact for external consumers).

## Done

- Schema definition exists in code with serde derives
- Serializer is wired into the ring buffer in crates/frankenlibc-membrane/src/evidence.rs
- Parser exists in crates/frankenlibc-harness
- Round-trip test green
- Trace_id propagates through a representative ABI call (verified by integration test)
close_reason: Stdio evidence schema/parser/ring buffer are implemented and verified: rch cargo test -p frankenlibc-harness --test stdio_evidence_schema passed 4/4.

title: ci: glibc version matrix (2.31, 2.34, 2.38) for NativeFile field-offset assertions
description:
## Background

Plan v4 ┬¦3. NativeFile is layout-compatible with glibcs _IO_FILE for the first 216 bytes. The pinned offsets are taken from glibc 2.34 (the dominant Ubuntu LTS version). But binaries built against earlier or later glibc may have slightly different offsets if upstream silently rearranged. CI must verify the offsets are correct on the relevant distro versions.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile layout)

## What to do

1. Add a CI matrix entry that runs the field-offset assertion test against:
   - glibc 2.31 (Ubuntu 20.04, RHEL 8)
   - glibc 2.34 (Ubuntu 22.04, baseline)
   - glibc 2.38 (Ubuntu 24.04, RHEL 9)
2. Use a simple Docker image per row, or buildjet matrix if available.
3. The test inspects /usr/include/bits/types/struct_FILE.h on the runner, extracts the field offsets via offsetof() in a small C program, and compares against the pinned constants in NativeFile.
4. CI fails if any offset diverges. Divergence triggers a Phase 5 refinement round on bd-9chy.1.
5. Document the policy in STDIO_NATIVE_REPLACEMENT_PLAN.md ┬¦3 (already there) and reference this CI matrix.

## Done

- CI matrix has 3 glibc rows
- Each runs the offset-extraction test
- All 3 are green at v0
- Documented in plan and CI configuration

title: test: NativeFile layout ŌĆö comprehensive offset assertion + size + alignment + glibc-version-matrix unit suite
description:
## Background

bd-9chy.1 implements the _IO_FILE_plus layout. This bead is the comprehensive test surface for that work. Separated because the test list is long enough to warrant its own tracking.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (the layout itself)

## Test list

1. Field-offset assertions for every glibc 2.34 _IO_FILE field via memoffset::offset_of! macro pinned to numeric constants extracted from /usr/include/bits/types/struct_FILE.h. Fields: _flags, _IO_read_ptr, _IO_read_end, _IO_read_base, _IO_write_base, _IO_write_ptr, _IO_write_end, _IO_buf_base, _IO_buf_end, _IO_save_base, _IO_backup_base, _IO_save_end, _markers, _chain, _fileno, _flags2, _old_offset, _cur_column, _vtable_offset, _shortbuf, _lock, _offset, _codecvt, _wide_data, _freeres_list, _freeres_buf, _pad5, _mode, _unused2.
2. size_of::<NativeFile>() <= 4096 (slab compat).
3. align_of::<NativeFile>() >= align_of::<*mut c_void>() (vtable alignment).
4. The vtable field offset is exactly at offset 216 (end of _IO_FILE region).
5. The reentrant mutex is positioned AFTER the vtable so a foreign pointer reading the first 216 bytes does not accidentally pull mutex bytes.
6. mbstate_t is initialized to the C-locale initial state on construction.
7. Generation counter starts at a fresh non-zero value (test that two consecutive constructions produce different generations).
8. The fingerprint is a function of (fd, generation, address) ŌĆö verify two NativeFiles with same fd but different generation have distinct fingerprints.
9. The orientation field default is Undecided (0).
10. Multi-glibc CI matrix (delegated to bd-9chy.41): all assertions hold on glibc 2.31, 2.34, 2.38.
11. Fuzz: cargo fuzz target that constructs NativeFiles with random parameters and checks the invariants in ┬¦1.5 of plan v4.

## Done

- All 11 test categories implemented as #[test]s in crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/native_file_layout_test.rs
- Multi-glibc CI matrix from bd-9chy.41 runs them
- Fuzz target compiles and runs for at least 60 seconds in CI without finding violations
close_reason: done

title: test: fopen/fdopen comprehensive unit + integration suite (mode parsing, isatty, errno, fd lifecycle, EINTR)
description:
## Background

bd-9chy.5 lists only one unit test in its done criteria. This bead is the comprehensive surface.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.5

## Test list

Mode parsing:
1. fopen("/tmp/x", "r") ŌåÆ O_RDONLY
2. fopen("/tmp/x", "w") ŌåÆ O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, mode 0666
3. fopen("/tmp/x", "a") ŌåÆ O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_APPEND
4. fopen("/tmp/x", "r+") ŌåÆ O_RDWR
5. fopen("/tmp/x", "w+") ŌåÆ O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC
6. fopen("/tmp/x", "a+") ŌåÆ O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_APPEND
7. Each of the above with trailing "b" (binary, no-op on Linux)
8. Each of the above with trailing "x" (O_EXCL ŌĆö fail if exists)
9. Trailing "e" (O_CLOEXEC) honored
10. Bad mode strings ŌåÆ NULL + EINVAL: "", "z", "rw", "ar", "rwe"

Path handling:
11. Long path (PATH_MAX) accepted
12. Path with spaces accepted
13. Path with embedded null ŌåÆ EINVAL
14. NULL path ŌåÆ EFAULT (or segfault ŌĆö POSIX is permissive; document choice)

isatty buffering:
15. fopen("/dev/tty", "w") on a tty ŌåÆ buffer mode = _IOLBF
16. fopen("/dev/null", "w") ŌåÆ buffer mode = _IOFBF
17. fopen("/tmp/x", "w") on a regular file ŌåÆ _IOFBF

errno preservation:
18. Failed fopen sets errno appropriately (ENOENT, EACCES, EISDIR)
19. Successful fopen does NOT touch errno

Fd lifecycle:
20. Returned NativeFile fd is a fresh fd (not 0/1/2)
21. fclose closes the fd (verify with fstat returning EBADF after)
22. fclose called twice ŌåÆ second call fails with EINVAL (or detects double-close ŌĆö POMDP healing in hardened mode)

fdopen:
23. fdopen of a fresh socket fd ŌåÆ NativeFile that supports read/write
24. fdopen of -1 ŌåÆ NULL + EBADF
25. fdopen with mode mismatching the fd open flags ŌåÆ EINVAL

EINTR:
26. fopen on a slow filesystem (LD_PRELOAD a slow openat) returning EINTR ŌåÆ retried transparently

## Done

- All 26 tests in tests/integration/fopen_fdopen_test.rs
- Each runs under both strict and hardened modes
- Tests use the real .so via LD_PRELOAD
notes: Comprehensive fopen/fdopen tests exist in stdio_abi_test.rs. Unit tests requiring NativeFile streams are marked as needing LD_PRELOAD integration. 98 tests pass, 25 appropriately ignored for vtable validation constraints.

title: stdio: rewrite fopen/fopen64/fdopen to be native-only, returning *mut NativeFile
description:
## Background

The audit pinpoints these as the only unconditional host delegators in stdio_abi.rs. fopen at L676-729 calls host_fopen() under prefer_host_stdio_streams() and otherwise falls back to native openat + registry insert. fdopen at L4060-4108 same pattern. Every other stdio function downstream "checks registry first; delegates to host if alien" ŌĆö meaning the *only* way alien pointers enter the system is through these two constructors. Eliminate the alien-pointer source and the rest of the cleanup follows naturally.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile must be _IO_FILE_plus-shaped before fopen/fdopen can return it without breaking existing pointer-arith assumptions).

## What to do

1. In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdio_abi.rs, rewrite fopen() (L676-729):
   - Parse mode string into open flags (existing helper).
   - Call native openat raw syscall (already at L703-709 in the fallback branch ŌĆö promote to primary).
   - Allocate a NativeFile from the slab allocator (or via the registry constructor ŌĆö verify which exists).
   - Initialize: fd, vtable=&NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T, generation=fresh, mbstate=initial, orientation=Undecided, lock_count=0, open_flags=oflags, fingerprint=fresh siphash.
   - Set buffering mode based on isatty() raw syscall (NOT libc::isatty): _IOLBF if isatty, _IOFBF otherwise.
   - Insert into stream registry.
   - Return as *mut FILE (cast). NEVER call host_fopen.
2. fopen64 is an alias to fopen on 64-bit platforms ŌĆö verify it stays that way and routes to the native path.
3. fdopen at L4060-4108 same surgery: take the input fd, build a NativeFile shell, set buffering mode based on isatty(fd), insert into registry, return.
4. Remove the prefer_host_stdio_streams() check from both functions. The function itself stays for now (deleted in a separate cleanup bead) but these two callers must not consult it.
5. Confirm the existing native-fallback branch in fopen (L694-729) becomes the primary and only branch.

## Cross-cutting

- The slab allocator for NativeFile should be a fixed-size pool to keep alloc/free deterministic. Coordinate with the malloc crate maintainer.
- isatty must be implemented as a raw ioctl(TCGETS) or fcntl probe ŌĆö using libc::isatty would re-introduce host delegation.

## Done

- fopen/fopen64/fdopen do not call host_*_fn() under any branch
- A unit test calls fopen("/dev/null", "w"), inspects the returned pointer, confirms its in the registry, and verifies the vtable pointer is set
- Existing fopen tests still pass
- The fopen body has zero references to prefer_host_stdio_streams or host_fopen_fn
close_reason: Rewrote fopen/fopen64/fdopen to use native NativeFile path. No host delegation. Returns real *mut NativeFile pointers. Uses raw syscalls for isatty check. Registers in native_stream_registry.

title: stdio: implement popen/pclose natively via posix_spawn + native fdopen
description:
## Background

popen is FILE-acquisition channel #3 in plan v4 ┬¦1.10. Today it lives in process_abi.rs or stdio_abi.rs (verify) and almost certainly delegates to host libc since host pipe+fork+fdopen is non-trivial to replicate. After the fopen/fdopen bead lands (bd-9chy.5), popen must build a native NativeFile around the pipe end.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (layout), bd-9chy.5 (native fdopen exists)

## What to do

1. Locate the current popen implementation. Likely in crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/process_abi.rs.
2. Replace with: native pipe2 raw syscall, native posix_spawn raw syscall (or fork+execve fallback for kernels without posix_spawn), close the unused pipe end in the parent, pass mode "r" or "w" to native fdopen on the surviving end.
3. pclose: read fd from the NativeFile, close it via native fclose path, then waitpid on the recorded child pid, return the child status. Store the child pid in a NativeFile extension field (add a popen_child_pid: Option<i32> to NativeFileState).
4. Both "r" and "w" modes must work; "re" (close-on-exec) must be honored.
5. An integration test runs `popen("echo hello", "r")`, fgets a line, pclose, asserts return == 0 and the line == "hello\n".

## Edge cases

- SIGCHLD handling: do not install a default SIG_IGN that would orphan zombies; let the user decide.
- popen inside a multi-threaded program: pthread_atfork handlers (separate bead bd-9chy-22 in the locking tier) must run before fork.
- Failure modes: spawn failure must close both pipe ends and return NULL with errno set.

## Done

- popen/pclose are native; no host delegation
- Both modes work; "re" honored
- Integration test passes
- popen_child_pid field added to NativeFileState

title: stdio: implement tmpfile/tmpfile64 natively via O_TMPFILE + native fdopen
description:
## Background

Channel #4 in plan v4 ┬¦1.10. POSIX tmpfile creates an unnamed temporary file that is auto-deleted on close. Linux >= 3.11 provides O_TMPFILE for atomic creation of an unnamed file in a directory; older kernels need mkstemp + unlink.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1, bd-9chy.5

## What to do

1. Locate current tmpfile in stdio_abi.rs (verify).
2. Implement: try openat(AT_FDCWD, "/tmp", O_TMPFILE|O_RDWR, 0600). If succeeded, build a NativeFile around the fd via native fdopen path.
3. Fallback for older kernels: use a fresh mkstemp template (e.g. /tmp/franken-tmp-XXXXXX), open O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_EXCL, then immediately unlink the path while keeping the fd open. Build NativeFile around it.
4. tmpfile64 is the alias on 64-bit platforms; verify it routes to the same code.
5. Test: tmpfile, fwrite "hello", fseek SEEK_SET, fread back, assert "hello", fclose. Verify the file is gone after fclose by trying to readdir /tmp for any leftover.

## Done

- O_TMPFILE primary path; mkstemp+unlink fallback
- No libc::tmpfile or host_*_fn references
- Integration test passes including the no-leftover check
close_reason: Already implemented: tmpfile uses O_TMPFILE primary + mkstemp/unlink fallback + fdopen_native_impl; tmpfile64 alias. 6/6 stdio tmpfile tests pass.

title: stdio: complete native fmemopen/open_memstream/open_wmemstream with NativeFile memory backing
description:
## Background

Channels #5/#6/#7 in plan v4 ┬¦1.10. The audit suggests these may be partially native already in crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdio/file.rs (which has mem_backing in StdioStream). This bead is the cleanup-and-completion pass.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1, bd-9chy.5

## What to do

1. Add a NativeFileBacking enum to NativeFileState: Fd(i32), MemoryFixed { buf: *mut u8, size: usize, owns: bool }, MemoryGrowing { buf: *mut *mut u8, size: *mut usize, capacity: usize }, Cookie { ... }.
2. fmemopen(buf, size, mode):
   - mode parsing identical to fopen
   - if buf is NULL, allocate a fresh size-byte buffer (owns=true); else use the caller buffer (owns=false)
   - the NativeFile carries Backing::MemoryFixed
   - reads return bytes from the buffer up to size; writes append until size, then return short with EOF set
   - "a" mode positions at first NUL or at size, whichever first
3. open_memstream(bufp, sizep):
   - allocate an initial 4 KiB growing buffer
   - on every fwrite that overflows, realloc to 2x capacity
   - on fclose or fflush, write the current buffer pointer and size to the user-provided bufp/sizep
   - the buffer must remain valid after fclose; only the NativeFile is freed
4. open_wmemstream(bufp, sizep): same as open_memstream but the orientation field is set to Wide at construction and locked.
5. All three must support the recursive lock (when bd-9chy locking bead lands).
6. Tests: round-trip write and read for all three; growing case for open_memstream that crosses the realloc boundary; orientation lock for open_wmemstream rejects byte-oriented operations (or auto-fwides under hardened mode).

## Done

- All three constructors live in stdio_abi.rs and route to the same native NativeFile path
- NativeFileBacking enum carries the memory backing variants
- Tests cover happy path, growing case, orientation locking
- No host delegation

title: Track D: Implement gai_cancel/gai_error/gai_suspend as synchronous-only stubs with proper error returns
description:
## Goal
Replace 3 async DNS stub functions in unistd_abi.rs (lines 17404-17423) that incorrectly return EAI_SYSTEM/ENOSYS with correct POSIX error returns.

## Critical Design Insight
FrankenLibC does NOT export getaddrinfo_a() ŌĆö the function that creates async DNS requests. Therefore, gai_error/gai_cancel/gai_suspend will NEVER receive valid in-flight request handles. The correct implementation is NOT a thread pool ŌĆö it is simply returning the correct error code for 'no such request' or 'no pending requests'.

## Current State (WRONG)
All 3 functions set errno=ENOSYS and return EAI_SYSTEM. This is incorrect because:
- gai_cancel should return EAI_NOCANCEL (not a valid request) or EAI_ALLDONE
- gai_error should return EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL (invalid gaicb)
- gai_suspend with an empty/null list should return EAI_ALLDONE (no pending requests)

Programs that probe async DNS availability by calling gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) get EAI_SYSTEM instead of EAI_ALLDONE, which may cause them to disable async features unnecessarily.

## Correct Implementation (SIMPLE)
### gai_cancel(req)
- If req is NULL: return EAI_NOCANCEL
- Otherwise: return EAI_ALLDONE (no async requests exist, so it is already 'done')

### gai_error(req)
- If req is NULL: set errno=EINVAL, return EAI_SYSTEM
- Otherwise: return 0 (treat any gaicb as 'completed' since we resolve synchronously)

### gai_suspend(list, nitems, timeout)
- If list is NULL or nitems <= 0: return 0 (nothing to wait for)
- Scan list entries: if all non-NULL entries have ar_result set, return 0
- If timeout is NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (would block forever waiting for nothing)
- If timeout is not NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (timeout immediately since no async work pending)

## Why This Is Correct
The POSIX spec says gai_suspend waits for 'at least one request to complete.' Since FrankenLibC resolves all getaddrinfo calls synchronously, any gaicb passed to gai_suspend has ALREADY been resolved by the time gai_suspend is called. The correct behavior is to check if the requests are done and return immediately.

## Testing
- Unit test: gai_cancel(NULL) returns EAI_NOCANCEL
- Unit test: gai_cancel(non_null_ptr) returns EAI_ALLDONE
- Unit test: gai_error(NULL) returns EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL
- Unit test: gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) returns 0
- Unit test: gai_suspend(empty_list, 0, NULL) returns 0
- e2e: scripts/check_gai_async_stubs.sh verifying correct return codes via small C test

## Structured Logging
All 3 functions must emit structured trace on invocation:
  trace_id, mode, api_family=resolv, symbol=gai_cancel|gai_error|gai_suspend, decision_path, errno, latency_ns

## Acceptance Criteria
All 3 functions return correct POSIX error codes. No ENOSYS returns. Tests pass. Clippy clean. e2e script passes.

## Note
If getaddrinfo_a() is ever implemented in the future, these functions would need to be upgraded to poll a real request registry. But that is a separate epic (P4+) since async DNS is extremely rare.
close_reason: Replaced incorrect ENOSYS returns with correct POSIX error codes. gai_cancel: NULL->EAI_NOTCANCELED, non-null->EAI_ALLDONE (all sync, nothing to cancel). gai_error: NULL->EAI_SYSTEM/EINVAL, non-null->0 (synchronous resolver means complete). gai_suspend: nitems<=0->0, otherwise->0 (all requests already resolved synchronously). No thread pool needed since getaddrinfo_a is not exported. Clippy clean.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Risk: gai_* async DNS used by almost no programs. ENOSYS acceptable for 99%+ workloads. If too complex, fallback: implement gai_error as immediate-return (EAI_ALLDONE for completed, EAI_NOCANCEL for unknown) and gai_suspend as no-wait poll. This synchronous stub passes most programs checking async DNS availability.

title: PORT: readpassphrase (OpenBSD passphrase-reader with flags)
description: OpenBSD readpassphrase(prompt, buf, bufsiz, flags) reads a passphrase from /dev/tty (or stdin/stderr with RPP_STDIN flag) into the caller-supplied buffer with echo disabled by default. Companion to the existing getpass shim but supports caller-provided storage (rather than thread-local) and flag-controlled behavior: RPP_ECHO_OFF/ON, RPP_REQUIRE_TTY, RPP_FORCELOWER/UPPER, RPP_SEVENBIT, RPP_STDIN. Used by sshd, sudo, ssh, GPG, every passphrase-prompting tool. Currently missing entirely from frankenlibc-abi. Implementation: thin shim modeled on getpass that opens /dev/tty (or stdin per RPP_STDIN), uses ioctl TCGETS/TCSETS to toggle ECHO based on RPP_ECHO_ON, reads byte-by-byte until newline, applies the case/seven-bit transformations, and restores echo on exit.
close_reason: shipped: unistd_abi::readpassphrase modeled on getpass with full OpenBSD flag set; 9 tests via piped stdin; clippy-clean; rch-built

title: [HIGH] pthread_key_create destructor not invoked on thread exit (deterministic)
description:
## Symptom

cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_tsd_test has a deterministic failure:

```
test tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit ... FAILED
---- tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit stdout ----
thread 'tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit' panicked at crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/pthread_tsd_test.rs:331:
assertion `left == right` failed: thread exit should run the registered TSD destructor
  left: 0
 right: 17476

test result: FAILED. 13 passed; 1 failed
```

17476 = 0x4444, the context value the child thread stored via pthread_setspecific.

## Test flow (pthread_tsd_test.rs:295)

1. pthread_key_create(&mut key, Some(record_tsd_destructor)) ŌåÆ rc 0.
2. Child thread set ctx.value (0x4444) via pthread_setspecific(key, ptr).
3. Child thread exits by returning from tsd_roundtrip_start (i.e. normal thread exit ŌĆö does not pthread_exit(3p) explicitly).
4. Main thread pthread_joins the child; retval matches ctx.value (this assertion passes, line 329).
5. Main thread reads TSD_DESTRUCTOR_LAST; expects it to contain 0x4444 because the registered destructor record_tsd_destructor() should have been called during child-thread teardown and stored the value it was handed. Actual value: 0. Destructor never ran.

## Diagnosis

POSIX and glibc specify that pthread_key_create-registered destructors are invoked during thread exit for every (key, value) pair whose value is non-NULL ŌĆö **in up to PTHREAD_DESTRUCTOR_ITERATIONS passes** to drain re-registered keys. Our teardown path is not calling the registered destructor at all.

Candidate causes:

1. The TSD destructor table isn't being iterated during native-thread exit. Either the native-thread exit path doesn't walk the pthread_key_create-registered destructor list, or the list is wiped before the walk.
2. The child thread's TSD cell for this key is NULL at exit time. (pthread_setspecific may have failed silently? But the prior assertion at line 329 shows ctx.value round-trips through retval, which uses the same storage.)
3. The destructor is stored per key globally but keyed on a per-thread index that doesn't match what pthread_setspecific wrote.

## Fix direction

Read crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs for pthread_key_create and the native-thread exit code path. Verify that:

- pthread_key_create stores the destructor function pointer per key.
- The native thread's exit routine walks every registered key, reads the thread's TSD slot for it, and, if non-NULL, sets the slot to NULL and calls the destructor with the old value.
- The walk runs before the thread's TLS storage is freed.

The authoritative reference is pthreads(7) and POSIX.1-2017 ┬¦2.9.2 'Thread-Specific Data'.

## Blast radius

Deterministic single test failure. Stale TSD from prior threads could leak memory in library users that rely on pthread_key_create destructors (e.g., every sensible C runtime's locale / errno / resolv-context TLS). This is also what a581f0ac / e289f838 were trying to get right on the __cxa_thread_atexit_impl side; the pthread_key TSD side appears to be a separate path that wasn't touched.

## Provenance

Found by cc-libc FULL REVIEW MODE at HEAD d485e53d br-bd-6kwnr: accept host condattr clocks. No SIGABRT, no parallel-race dependency ŌĆö reproduces on isolated cargo test run of this single test.
notes:
## Bisection by cc-libc (2026-04-22 review-mode pass)

The core TSD logic works correctly in isolation ŌĆö the unit test
`pthread::tls::tests::destructor_called_on_teardown`
(crates/frankenlibc-core/src/pthread/tls.rs:738) passes cleanly when run
directly: `cargo test -p frankenlibc-core --lib pthread::tls::tests::destructor_called_on_teardown`
ŌåÆ 1 passed, 0 failed.

That unit test exercises: pthread_key_create(key, dtor) ŌåÆ register_thread_tls(tid, ptr) ŌåÆ
pthread_setspecific(key, val) ŌåÆ teardown_thread_tls(tid) ŌåÆ destructor invoked. When driven
directly against the core/src/pthread/tls.rs API, every step works. So the bug is NOT in:
 - KEY_REGISTRY_RCU registration / destructor storage (works in unit test).
 - teardown_thread_tls walk (works in unit test).
 - The seq + in_use check (works in unit test).
 - FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES vs table path (works in unit test).

That narrows the bug to the ABI / clone-trampoline integration path:

 - `pthread_abi::pthread_key_create` (pthread_abi.rs:2792) wraps core_pthread_key_create via
   with_threading_policy_guard. Both fallback and work closures call the core fn. Not
   ruled out but looks benign.
 - `pthread_abi::pthread_setspecific` (pthread_abi.rs:2868) same pattern, also calls
   core_pthread_setspecific in both closures.
 - `core/src/pthread/thread.rs` trampoline at line 170-241: calls register_thread_tls at
   line 228 with handle.tid.load(Ordering::Acquire), and exit_current_thread (line 241) ŌåÆ
   teardown_thread_tls(sys_gettid()) at line 319.
 - CLONE_PARENT_SETTID + CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID interaction at thread.rs:546-548 ŌĆö handle.tid
   holds the real TID until the child exits (CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID only fires on exit).

## Strongest hypothesis

handle.tid at register_thread_tls time (line 228 ŌĆö BEFORE start_routine runs) is
`handle.tid.load(Acquire)`. The parent's CLONE_PARENT_SETTID wrote the child's real TID
there, and the comment at thread.rs:564-568 says 'CLONE_PARENT_SETTID already published
tid into handle.tid' and warns that parent should NOT write tid again because
CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID can clear it back to 0. So when the child's trampoline reaches line
228 it reads handle.tid which is the child's real TID.

But the code at thread.rs:190-194 shows the child's trampoline ALSO runs:

```
    let current_tid = syscall::sys_gettid();
    handle.self_tid.store(current_tid, Ordering::Release);
    if handle.tid.load(Ordering::Acquire) == 0 {
        handle.tid.store(current_tid, Ordering::Release);
    }
```

If we're somehow in the branch where `handle.tid.load() == 0` (maybe clone race on
short-lived child?), this stores the real TID. If NOT, handle.tid has the parent's view.
Either way, at line 228 handle.tid should equal sys_gettid().

EXCEPT: if CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID fires before register_thread_tls (unlikely ŌĆö we're still
pre-exit), OR if the child's sys_gettid() returns a different TID than CLONE_PARENT_SETTID
wrote (would need kernel bug).

## Suggested fix direction

Add an eprintln! to both register_thread_tls and teardown_thread_tls printing tid and
non-null status, then run the failing integration test. Compare: does register see tid X
with valid ptr, and does teardown see the same tid X with a valid ptr? If yes ŌåÆ the walk
executes but the slot state is wrong. If no ŌåÆ tid mismatch between the two calls.

Alternative: maybe the failing path actually goes through
`dispatch_host_thread_create_with_managed_attr` instead of native_pthread_create, because
`lock_and_force_native` sets a thread-local override that doesn't bleed through to
FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING global. Verify by stepping pthread_create and checking which
branch of the `if !force_native && let Some(host_create) = resolved_thread_create_raw()`
guard is taken. If the test is actually using the HOST pthread_create, register_thread_tls
is never called, table_lookup(tid) returns null, write_tls_value falls back to
FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (thread-local Cell), and teardown_thread_tls early-returns because
table_lookup returns null ŌĆö destructor never fires. This is consistent with the observed
symptoms: setspecific+getspecific round-trip works (via FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES) but
destructor doesn't fire (because teardown only walks the table path).

That's the most likely explanation. The fix is either:
1. Have write_tls_value to FALLBACK also register the thread in the table so teardown
   finds it, OR
2. Have teardown_thread_tls ALSO walk FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES (it's thread-local so it's
   actually perfectly addressable from inside the exiting thread), OR
3. Fix the test setup to ensure force_native propagates such that pthread_create uses
   native_pthread_create (which DOES call register_thread_tls).

Option 2 is the most robust ŌĆö thread-exit can always walk FALLBACK because it's
thread-local to the exiting thread, and if any values were stored there, the destructor
should fire.

## FIXED by cc-libc (2026-04-22)

Root cause: pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests() only set the THREAD-LOCAL FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING_OVERRIDE. The tests use `FORCE_NATIVE_ONCE: std::sync::Once` to call it, but `Once::call_once` runs the setter on exactly one thread (whichever cargo-test worker tripped it first). Every OTHER worker thread saw FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING_OVERRIDE = None and fell back to the global atomic FORCE_NATIVE_THREADING, which was never stored true and defaulted to false. So on every worker thread except one, pthread_create routed through the `!force_native` branch into the host libc path (dispatch_host_thread_create_with_managed_attr) ŌĆö register_thread_tls was never called for the child, teardown_thread_tls never ran at child exit, and the registered TSD destructor was never invoked. The setspecific + getspecific round-trip asserts passed because write_tls_value's fallback path (FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES thread-local Cell) works for the same thread's read.

Fix: set both the global atomic AND the thread-local override in pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests(). Now the first worker to trip FORCE_NATIVE_ONCE flips the global for all subsequent tests/workers.

Also landed a defensive teardown_thread_tls improvement: the walk now falls through to FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES when the TID has no table entry, so any future code path that stores specifics on a non-registered thread still gets its destructor invoked at thread exit (FALLBACK_TLS_VALUES is thread-local so this is safe ŌĆö only the exiting thread can reach it).

Validation:
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_tsd_test -> 14 passed, 0 failed
  (previously 13 passed, 1 failed on tsd_destructor_runs_on_thread_exit).
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-core --lib pthread -> 152 passed, 0 failed.
- cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test pthread_abi_test -> 180 passed, 0 failed (regression-tested the pthread surface ŌĆö no breakage from the global-atomic store).
close_reason: Fixed: pthread_threading_force_native_for_tests now sets global atomic, not just thread-local override.

title: gethostbyname_r/gethostbyaddr_r clobber h_errno on ERANGE (glibc parity)
description:
Cod's bd-[-] fix (commit af29f73) fixed gethostbyname2_r to preserve h_errno on ERANGE (glibc contract: h_errno untouched when caller buffer too small). The same bug exists in two adjacent functions:

1. gethostbyname_r_impl (resolv_abi.rs line ~1558): on write_reentrant_hostent Err, unconditionally sets h_errnop=NO_RECOVERY_ERRNO and returns the code ŌĆö clobbers h_errno even when code==ERANGE.

2. gethostbyaddr_r_impl (resolv_abi.rs line ~1628): identical pattern.

Fix: on ERANGE specifically, leave h_errnop untouched. Add tests mirroring cod's gethostbyname2_r_ipv6_small_buffer_preserves_h_errno.
close_reason: Fixed h_errno-clobber-on-ERANGE in gethostbyname_r_impl and gethostbyaddr_r_impl in commit 3089d44. Both now guard the h_errnop write by code != ERANGE, matching cod's gethostbyname2_r fix (af29f73). Renamed existing hardcoded-bug test to assert glibc-parity sentinel preservation; added gethostbyaddr_r_small_buffer_returns_erange_preserves_h_errno for the symmetric reverse-lookup path.

title: bd-2ry subtask: Deterministic fixture pack for nested/edge non-local jump scenarios
description:
Background:
- setjmp correctness must be demonstrated via integration fixtures, not just unit tests.

Goal:
- Add deterministic fixture pack for non-local control transfer, including adversarial edge cases.

Deliverables:
1) Fixture programs for nested jumps and error handling paths.
2) Strict+hardened expected-output profiles.
3) Failure artifact capture and triage docs.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Fixture suite passes in declared supported scope.
- Any unsupported scenario fails with explicit documented semantics.

Verification & Logging:
- E2E scripts with deterministic replay controls.
- Structured logs: trace_id, scenario_id, mode, jump_depth, mask_state, outcome, timing, artifact_refs.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

### Mandatory Verification Gate
1. Unit Test Evidence
- Add deterministic unit tests for nominal, boundary, and invalid-input paths in the touched modules.
- Include strict and hardened mode assertions when behavior differs by runtime mode.
- Include failure-mode tests covering repair/deny/default behavior with explicit expected outputs.

2. Integration + E2E Evidence
- Add or extend fixture-driven integration coverage in `tests/conformance` and/or `tests/integration` for this bead scope.
- Add a deterministic E2E script path (or harness command) with explicit setup/teardown and replayable seeds.
- Compare FrankenLibC outputs against host glibc fixtures (or documented replacement-level contract) for all touched symbols.

3. Logging + Traceability Evidence
- Emit structured logs (JSONL or deterministic text) that include bead ID, scenario ID, runtime mode, symbol family, decision path, and outcome.
- Verify logs expose repair/deny counters and reason codes so regressions are diagnosable from artifacts alone.
- Record artifact paths in bead notes (test logs, fixture diffs, benchmark outputs, conformance reports).

4. Performance + Regression Evidence
- Capture before/after baseline for latency/throughput on touched hot paths.
- Enforce no-regression threshold or document bounded tradeoff with quantitative rationale.

5. CI Gate Evidence
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo check --all-targets`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test --all-targets`
- all bead-specific harness and E2E scripts pass with deterministic logs archived.
notes:
## Mandatory Test/E2E Logging Checklist
- [ ] Unit tests added/updated for happy-path, edge-case, and invalid-input behavior.
- [ ] Property-style or fuzz-seed replay coverage added where state space is large/combinatorial.
- [ ] Integration fixtures added/updated in `tests/conformance` and validated against host glibc expectations.
- [ ] Deterministic E2E script/runner added or updated with explicit command lines and seed controls.
- [ ] Strict vs hardened behavior assertions are explicit and versioned in fixtures.
- [ ] Structured logs include: timestamp, bead ID, test scenario, runtime mode, symbol family, decision path, action (allow/repair/deny), and result.
- [ ] Failure logs include reason codes + minimal reproducible input payload metadata.
- [ ] Benchmark/perf logs include baseline, candidate, delta %, and pass/fail gate decision.
- [ ] CI artifacts include conformance report, E2E transcript, and summarized pass/fail matrix.
- [ ] Release/merge gate blocked if any required test or log artifact is missing.

title: EPIC: Dynamic System Constants ŌĆö Replace hard-coded values with runtime queries
description:
FrankenLibC currently hard-codes several system-dependent constants (page size, stack limits, group counts, FD limits) that should be queried at runtime for cross-architecture correctness.

BACKGROUND: While FrankenLibC currently targets x86_64 Linux, these hard-coded values create latent bugs that will surface when porting to ARM64 (16KB/64KB pages), RISC-V, or systems with non-standard configurations. Even on x86_64, some values (like supplementary group count) can differ from defaults.

SCOPE: This epic covers all hard-coded constants identified in the comprehensive audit across malloc_abi.rs, unistd_abi.rs, glibc_internal_abi.rs, stdlib_abi.rs, pthread_abi.rs, and process_abi.rs.

ALREADY DONE: sysconf(_SC_PAGESIZE) now queries AT_PAGESZ from /proc/self/auxv at runtime (cached). The mmap_alloc bootstrap path intentionally keeps 4096 as the minimum alignment (documented).

RATIONALE: AGENTS.md says "We want to do things the RIGHT way with NO TECH DEBT" ŌĆö hard-coded system constants are textbook tech debt.
close_reason: Delivered: code + artifacts present.

title: Replace hard-coded 64-group limit with dynamic NGROUPS_MAX query
description:
FILE: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/glibc_internal_abi.rs:4592
CURRENT: let mut groups = [0u32; 64]
PROBLEM: NGROUPS_MAX can be 65536 on modern Linux. Hard-coding 64 silently truncates group membership for users in >64 groups, breaking permission checks.
FIX: Use a two-pass approach ŌĆö first call getgroups(0, NULL) to get count, then allocate dynamically. Or use sysconf(_SC_NGROUPS_MAX) to size the buffer.
IMPACT: Any program calling getgroups() or initgroups() for users in many groups will get truncated results.
CONSIDERATION: The Vec allocation here is fine since this isn't a bootstrap-critical path.

title: Replace hard-coded FD_SETSIZE=1024 with libc::FD_SETSIZE
description:
FILES: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/rpc_abi.rs:2283-2287, glibc_internal_abi.rs:3074, fortify_abi.rs:1001
CURRENT: Hard-coded bounds check !(0..1024) and [u8; 128] arrays (128 = 1024/8).
PROBLEM: While glibc itself uses FD_SETSIZE=1024, using the constant name is better than magic numbers. More importantly, the fd_set TLS buffer in rpc_abi.rs is sized to exactly 128 bytes ŌĆö should derive from libc::FD_SETSIZE / 8.
FIX: Replace magic number 1024 with libc::FD_SETSIZE constant. Replace 128-byte arrays with [u8; libc::FD_SETSIZE as usize / 8].
IMPACT: Medium ŌĆö affects programs using select() with fd > 1024, though poll/epoll is preferred.

title: Increase CPU affinity mask from 128 bytes (1024 CPUs) to support larger systems
description:
FILE: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:1804
CURRENT: let mut mask = [0u8; 128] ŌĆö supports max 1024 CPUs.
PROBLEM: Large servers can have >1024 logical CPUs (e.g., AMD EPYC with 256 cores + SMT = 512, multi-socket = 1024+). The mask should be sized to the actual system or use a reasonable upper bound.
FIX: Either use a larger static buffer (e.g., 512 bytes = 4096 CPUs) or dynamically query the kernel for the actual affinity mask size. The sched_getaffinity syscall returns the actual size used, so we could do a two-pass approach.
IMPACT: Low ŌĆö only affects very large servers. Current 1024 CPU limit is sufficient for most systems.

title: Make default thread stack size configurable via environment variable
description:
FILES: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs:2488,2494
CURRENT: ATTR_DEFAULT_STACK_SIZE = 2 * 1024 * 1024 (2 MiB), ATTR_MIN_STACK_SIZE = 16384 (16 KiB)
CONTEXT: glibc uses a default of 8 MiB, musl uses 128 KiB. Our 2 MiB is a reasonable middle ground but differs from both.
FIX: Keep 2 MiB as default but allow override via FRANKENLIBC_THREAD_STACK_SIZE env var. The minimum should match PTHREAD_STACK_MIN from the system.
IMPACT: Low ŌĆö 2 MiB works for most applications. Some embedded or memory-constrained scenarios might want smaller stacks.
CONSIDERATION: Don't read env var on every thread creation ŌĆö cache it at init time.

title: Fix _SC_TTY_NAME_MAX from 32 to match system reality
description:
FILE: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:1924
CURRENT: _SC_TTY_NAME_MAX = 32
PROBLEM: While glibc returns 32 for this, some systems have longer TTY names (especially for pseudo-terminals with long paths like /dev/pts/XXX). The constant should match glibc's behavior for compatibility.
FIX: Keep 32 to match glibc behavior but add a comment documenting the rationale. This is actually correct for glibc compat.
IMPACT: Very low ŌĆö TTY names are almost always < 32 chars.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Verified: _SC_TTY_NAME_MAX=32 matches glibc behavior. No change needed.

title: PORT: radixsort/sradixsort (NetBSD libutil radix sort family)
description: Port the NetBSD libutil radixsort(3) family: radixsort(base,nmemb,table,endbyte) and sradixsort(base,nmemb,table,endbyte). Both sort an array of pointers to byte strings (terminated by endbyte) using an optional 256-entry translation table that remaps each input byte to a sort key. sradixsort guarantees stable order of equal keys; radixsort does not. Implementation: pure-safe core radix_sort(items: &[&[u8]], table: Option<&[u8;256]>) returning a permutation Vec<usize> using stable sort with per-position translated-byte comparison; abi shims that walk each pointer to find endbyte, build slices, sort, and rewrite the pointer array in place. Tests: identity table, reverse-order table, NULL table = byte order, embedded-bytes preserved at endbyte boundary, stability for sradixsort, length-difference ordering, NULL/zero edge cases.
close_reason: shipped: radixsort + sradixsort with optional 256-byte translation table + endbyte sentinel + 7 core tests + 11 abi tests, all green

title: fuzz: pathname encoding edge cases (NUL, UTF-8, long, .. traversal, symlinks)
description:
## Scope
Add crates/frankenlibc-fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_pathname.rs targeting the pathname-parsing + canonicalization surface: realpath, canonicalize_file_name, basename/dirname, access/faccessat, stat/lstat/fstatat, readlink/readlinkat. These share a common pathname-bytes input channel and are the classic CVE surface (CVE-2018-1000001 realpath OOB, CVE-2016-1234 glibc glob).

## Archetype
1 + 3: Crash-detector on the impl + **differential** against libc for path operations that don't touch fs state (basename/dirname) and for the few that do (stat/readlink) over a pre-seeded tmpdir with a known symlink graph.

## Inputs
struct PathnameFuzzInput {
  op: u8,                 // 0=realpath, 1=basename, 2=dirname, 3=stat, 4=lstat, 5=readlink, 6=access
  bytes: Vec<u8>,         // arbitrary ŌĆö rejected if it contains interior NUL (CString won't build)
  buf_size: u16,          // for readlink
  flags: i32,             // for faccessat etc.
}

## Invariants
- Never panic
- For basename/dirname: result string length <= original + some constant; bytes are a suffix/prefix of the input
- For realpath: if non-null, resolved path has no '//' or '/./' or trailing '/', all components are canonical
- For stat family: non-negative rc implies st_dev/st_ino written; errno on failure is in the documented set
- Host parity: our errno family matches libc for inert paths

## Seed corpus
- empty, '/', '.', '..', '/tmp', '/proc/self', '/dev/null', '/etc/passwd', '/////' (collapsing slashes), '../../../..', path with unicode (├®, õĖŁ, emoji), path with high-bit bytes, path at NAME_MAX (255), path at PATH_MAX (4096)

## Expected bug classes
- PATH_MAX overflow, NAME_MAX overflow, NUL-embedded path, UTF-8 boundary split, symlink loop detection, '..' collapsing vs preserving, trailing-slash semantics, basename('') = '', dirname('/') = '/'

## Score
5 ├Ś 4 ├Ś /3 Ōēł 6.7 ŌĆö high (path CVEs are a historical goldmine).
close_reason: Added seeded pathname fuzz coverage, canonical path assertions, and host return/errno parity checks for basename, dirname, stat/lstat, access, fstatat, readlink/readlinkat, and faccessat.

title: Deterministic E2E suite v2: smoke/stress/fault/stability with replay
description:
Background:
- Passing unit/conformance tests is necessary but insufficient; final confidence requires deterministic end-to-end behavior under realistic programs and fault scenarios.

Goal:
- Build v2 deterministic e2e suite covering smoke, stress, fault-injection, and long-run stability in strict and hardened modes.

Deliverables:
1) Scenario catalog with required strict/hardened execution pairs.
2) Deterministic replay controls (fixed seeds, pinned env, scenario manifests).
3) Artifact capture for every run (logs, diffs, summaries, crash bundles where applicable).
4) CI integration with clear failure triage outputs.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Clean checkout executes all e2e scenarios reproducibly.
- Failures are diagnosable without rerunning interactively.
- E2E gates become a release blocker for target streams.

Test and Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for scenario manifest parsing and replay controls.
- E2E scripts per scenario class with strict+hardened assertions.
- Structured logs: trace_id, scenario_id, mode, subsystem, outcome, errno, timing, artifact_refs.

Extreme Optimization Requirements:
- Include perf-sensitive e2e scenarios to catch regressions that unit microbenches miss.

title: xdr_string/xdr_bytes/xdr_array DECODE allocate from attacker-supplied length BEFORE stream-bounds check
description:
## Reproduction

Found by fuzz_xdr (~10s of fuzzing) on a 16-byte stream:

```
Scenario {
  initial_op_mode: 1,        // XDR_DECODE
  buf_init: [253],           // 1 byte (resized to 16 in fuzz harness)
  buf_size_lie: 1828716579,  // (clamped to 16, doesn't matter)
  ops: [WrapstringDecode],
}
```

The first 4 bytes of the stream decode as the u32 length (RFC 4506 ┬¦4.11
big-endian). With `buf_init=[253]` resized to 16, the first 4 bytes are
`fd 00 00 00`, decoded as length = 0xfd000000 Ōēł 4.24 GB. xdr_string
allocates a 4.24 GB buffer via `libc::malloc` and only THEN passes it to
xdr_opaque, where xgb finally notices the stream only has 12 bytes
remaining and returns XDR_FALSE.

libFuzzer reports `oom-...` artifact and exit 71 (SIGOUT).

## Defect

In `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/rpc_abi.rs`:

- `xdr_string` (line ~1119): reads u32 size, validates `size <= maxsize`,
  malloc(size+1) ŌĆö `maxsize` is `c_uint::MAX` for callers using
  `xdr_wrapstring`, so any 32-bit attacker length passes.
- `xdr_bytes` (line ~1061): same pattern; malloc(size) with maxsize check
  only.
- `xdr_array` (line ~1204): malloc(count * elsize) with `count <=
  maxsize` and overflow check, but the element-count cap is also caller-
  supplied via maxsize.

The `maxsize` is the spec-level upper bound on the value, not the
wire-level upper bound on what can actually fit in the remaining
stream. Even with a sane caller-supplied maxsize, xdr_string lets an
attacker request `min(maxsize, 4GB)` of memory from a stream that
contains only a few bytes.

This is direct CVE-class:
- CVE-2017-17807 (rpcbind: xdr_array len * elsize OOM/DoS)
- CVE-2020-25645 (sunrpc xdr_bytes: malloc before bounds check)
- CVE-2022-23219 (sunrpc xdrmem_create + xdr_opaque length lie)

## Fix

In `xdr_string`, `xdr_bytes`, and `xdr_array` DECODE branches, BEFORE
calling libc::malloc, query the remaining stream bytes via the
xdr_inline / x_getpostn / x_handy field and reject any decoded
length whose payload (size + padding) cannot fit. This is defense-
in-depth on top of the existing maxsize check ŌĆö a malicious caller
that passes `maxsize=u32::MAX` (which `xdr_wrapstring` does
unconditionally per the glibc spec contract) still cannot trigger
OOM if the decoded length exceeds remaining stream bytes.

Implementation note: `Xdr::x_handy` for xdrmem streams stores the
remaining unread byte count. For xdrrec/xdrstdio streams the check
is harder (streaming source), but for those streams the wire
protocol is record-framed and `xdrrec_eof` / record-length checks
already provide some bounding.

## Severity

[HIGH] (P2). Pre-auth DoS for any service that exposes XDR-parsed
input from untrusted peers (RPC, NFS, NIS+, LDAP-over-XDR
deployments). Mitigation by caller-supplied maxsize is incomplete
because xdr_wrapstring (and any spec-following caller) passes
maxsize=u32::MAX.
notes: Fixed in f7581b5e: (1) xdrmem_remaining_bytes + xdr_padded_len helpers added; xdr_string/xdr_bytes/xdr_array DECODE paths now reject decoded lengths whose padded payload exceeds remaining xdrmem stream BEFORE libc::malloc, blocking the 4 GiB attacker-supplied length DoS vector. (2) xdr_array now rejects elsize=0 with c!=0, closing the 1-byte malloc + per-element 4-byte write heap-buffer-overflow. Verified: 85/85 xdr_abi_test pass, fuzz_xdr 1.6M runs in 91s (~17k exec/s) with no crashes. Found via fuzz_xdr in <60s.

title: PORT: humanize_number (NetBSD/FreeBSD libutil byte-count formatter)
description: NetBSD libutil humanize_number(buf, len, bytes, suffix, scale, flags) renders an int64 byte count into a human-readable string in buf, like "1.5K" or "32 MiB". Companion to dehumanize_number (just shipped). Six-prefix table (space K M G T P E) divided by 1024 by default or 1000 when HN_DIVISOR_1000 is set; switches to IEC prefixes (Ki Mi Gi ŌĆ”) when HN_IEC_PREFIXES is set. HN_AUTOSCALE picks the largest scale where the value still fits in three digits; HN_GETSCALE returns the auto-scale value without writing. HN_DECIMAL adds a single fractional digit when the integer part is < 10. HN_NOSPACE/HN_B control the spacing and trailing "B". Used by df(1), du(1), ls -h, and many BSD/Linux utilities. Ship: pure-safe core::stdlib::humanize_number with a HumanizeFlags bitmask + auto-scale algorithm + thin abi shim that maps the C int "scale" parameter to either a numeric scale or one of the special HN_AUTOSCALE/HN_GETSCALE sentinels.
close_reason: shipped: core::stdlib::humanize_number + stdlib_abi shim with HN_AUTOSCALE/HN_GETSCALE sentinel mapping; 23+12 tests; clippy-clean; rch-built

title: bd-c1x subtask: Condvar semantics spec (spurious wakeups, timeout clocks, errno rules)
description:
Background:
- Condition variables are subtle (spurious wakeups, timeout clocks, predicate loops) and require precise semantics before implementation.

Goal:
- Produce clean-room condvar semantics spec including clock handling and spurious-wakeup contract.

Deliverables:
1) wait/signal/broadcast/timedwait semantics table.
2) CLOCK_REALTIME vs CLOCK_MONOTONIC timeout conversion rules.
3) Spurious wakeup policy and required caller predicate-loop guidance.
4) Error mapping and invalid-usage handling.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Spec is implementation-ready and self-contained.
- Deferred/unsupported semantics explicitly documented.

Verification & Logging:
- Unit tests for timeout conversion helpers and semantic table consistency.
- Structured logs for spec-check tools and generated matrices.

comments.author: PurpleHawk
comments.text:
Completed condvar semantics spec (bd-blg). Deliverables:

1) cond_contract.md: Full transition table for init/destroy/wait/timedwait/signal/broadcast with state model (Uninitialized/Idle/Waiting/Destroyed).

2) cond.rs expanded with executable clean-room contract: CondvarContractState enum, CondvarContractOp enum, CondvarContractOutcome struct, condvar_contract_transition() const fn, CondvarAttributeContract with process_shared deferred, valid_timespec_nsec validator, futex_condvar_contention_note(), spurious_wakeup_policy().

3) 41 unit tests pass covering: all state x operation transitions, attribute support/deferral, timespec validation, lifecycle scenarios, signal/broadcast semantics, and policy documentation.

4) Conformance fixture: tests/conformance/fixtures/pthread_cond.json with 24 test cases covering all 6 operations, clock handling, null pointer checks, timeout semantics, mutex mismatch, spurious wakeup pattern, and lifecycle.

5) Spec explicitly addresses: CLOCK_REALTIME/MONOTONIC timeout conversion, spurious wakeup predicate-loop requirement, mutex association invariant (all concurrent waiters same mutex), futex requeue optimization for broadcast, and deferred features (process-shared).

Verification: cargo test -p frankenlibc-core -- pthread::cond (41 passed, 0 failed). cargo check --all-targets clean.

title: EPIC: Reality-check ambition closure program
description: Convert the reality-check ambition findings into an executable backlog that moves FrankenLibC from honest L0 interpose evidence toward evidence-gated replacement readiness. Validation gate: unit tests for touched logic, fixture or e2e coverage where externally visible, structured JSONL/log evidence with bead id/mode/symbol/outcome/artifact refs, and exact closure commands. acceptance_criteria:
Polish-pass acceptance criteria (applies before closure):
1. Preserve existing features, functions, artifacts, and ambition. Do not narrow scope unless a separate bead explicitly records the stronger replacement plan and evidence.
2. Implementation beads must include comprehensive unit tests for happy-path, boundary, invalid/adversarial, concurrency/stateful where relevant, and regression cases.
3. Externally visible behavior must include deterministic e2e, fixture, or harness scripts. Run strict and hardened modes where mode affects behavior; include L0/L1 replacement-level expectations where relevant.

7. Claims in README, FEATURE_PARITY, release notes, or replacement_levels.json cannot advance without current machine-verifiable evidence.
8. Before closing, verify br dep cycles is empty and record any pre-existing unrelated validation failures separately from bead-owned failures.

Acceptance:

acceptance_criteria:

1. Preserve all br and bv diagnostic surfaces; do not hide disagreements by choosing only one source unless the report explicitly records precedence and risk.
2. Define a discrepancy taxonomy for DB/JSONL count mismatch, exact-ID split-brain, timeout, stale blocked cache, already-shipped-but-open bead, conflicting ready lists, cycle-report disagreement, and missing issue/dependency records.
3. Produce a deterministic dashboard/report format that includes current source of truth, evidence age, affected bead IDs, user impact, next_safe_action, and whether implementation may proceed on unrelated beads.
4. Include unit tests for parser/report logic and negative tests for stale, contradictory, partial, and timeout-only inputs.
5. Include e2e-style replay transcripts using br and bv robot commands over a fixture or current snapshot, with detailed logs that separate tool failure from code or plan failure.
6. Logs must include trace_id, tracker_run_id, command, exit_status, duration_ms, source, bead_id, discrepancy_type, expected, actual, source_commit, artifact_refs, and failure_signature.
7. Closure must list exact br/bv commands, report artifact paths, known tracker limitations, and the next safe bead or repair bead for each disagreement class.

acceptance_criteria:
Polish-pass acceptance criteria (applies before closure):
1. Preserve existing features, functions, artifacts, and ambition. Do not narrow scope unless a separate bead explicitly records the stronger replacement plan and evidence.
2. Implementation beads must include comprehensive unit tests for happy-path, boundary, invalid/adversarial, concurrency/stateful where relevant, and regression cases.
3. Externally visible behavior must include deterministic e2e, fixture, or harness scripts. Run strict and hardened modes where mode affects behavior; include L0/L1 replacement-level expectations where relevant.
4. Logs must be structured JSONL or deterministic text with trace_id, bead_id, scenario_id, runtime_mode, replacement_level, api_family, symbol, oracle_kind, expected, actual, errno, decision_path, healing_action, latency_ns, artifact_refs, source_commit, target_dir, and failure_signature when applicable.
5. Closure evidence must name exact commands, expected outputs or thresholds, artifact paths, regenerated reports, and representative failure signatures. Use rch for heavy cargo gates with an isolated target directory.
6. User-facing work must include realistic workload evidence or explicitly justify why a unit/harness-only proof is sufficient.
7. Claims in README, FEATURE_PARITY, release notes, or replacement_levels.json cannot advance without current machine-verifiable evidence.
8. Before closing, verify br dep cycles is empty and record any pre-existing unrelated validation failures separately from bead-owned failures.

Use rch for heavy cargo gates and keep one-bead-at-a-time execution.
acceptance_criteria:
Polish-pass acceptance criteria (applies before closure):
1. Preserve existing features, functions, artifacts, and ambition. Do not narrow scope unless a separate bead explicitly records the stronger replacement plan and evidence.
2. Implementation beads must include comprehensive unit tests for happy-path, boundary, invalid/adversarial, concurrency/stateful where relevant, and regression cases.
3. Externally visible behavior must include deterministic e2e, fixture, or harness scripts. Run strict and hardened modes where mode affects behavior; include L0/L1 replacement-level expectations where relevant.
4. Logs must be structured JSONL or deterministic text with trace_id, bead_id, scenario_id, runtime_mode, replacement_level, api_family, symbol, oracle_kind, expected, actual, errno, decision_path, healing_action, latency_ns, artifact_refs, source_commit, target_dir, and failure_signature when applicable.
5. Closure evidence must name exact commands, expected outputs or thresholds, artifact paths, regenerated reports, and representative failure signatures. Use rch for heavy cargo gates with an isolated target directory.
6. User-facing work must include realistic workload evidence or explicitly justify why a unit/harness-only proof is sufficient.
7. Claims in README, FEATURE_PARITY, release notes, or replacement_levels.json cannot advance without current machine-verifiable evidence.
8. Before closing, verify br dep cycles is empty and record any pre-existing unrelated validation failures separately from bead-owned failures.

title: Build fixture coverage prioritizer for exported symbol families
description: Use support_matrix.json, symbol_fixture_coverage.v1.json, per_symbol_fixture_tests.v1.json, and real workload risk to rank fixture campaigns by coverage gain and parity risk. Output a machine-readable prioritized queue. acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance:
1. Preserve exported symbol-family coverage ambition; prioritization ranks work but must not erase low-priority uncovered symbols.
2. Define prioritizer inputs from version scripts, ABI exports, support_matrix, semantic overlay, fixture inventory, user workload matrix, hard-parts risk, and historical failures.
3. Include unit tests for ranking weights, missing data, duplicate symbols, stale fixture inventory, family grouping, and deterministic ordering.
4. Include deterministic e2e scripts regenerating priority artifacts from current inputs and proving stable output across runs.
5. Logs must include trace_id, bead_id, symbol_family, score, rank, coverage_state, risk_factors, artifact_refs, source_commit, and failure_signature.

close_reason: Implemented deterministic fixture coverage prioritizer generator and gate. Added generator self-tests for ranking weights, stale fixture inventory, duplicate per-symbol rows, and deterministic ordering; regenerated tests/conformance/fixture_coverage_prioritizer.v1.json from current inputs; gate emits structured report/log with trace_id, bead_id, symbol_family, score, rank, coverage_state, risk_factors, artifact_refs, source_commit, and failure_signature. Top families: unistd/process/filesystem, string/memory hotpaths, stdio/libio, pthread/sync, malloc/membrane. Counts: 18 campaigns, 18 deferred modules, 3346 selected uncovered target symbols, 382 deferred uncovered target symbols, 3728 all uncovered target symbols, 196 first-wave fixtures. Validated with py_compile, generator --self-test, generator --check, bash -n, check_fixture_coverage_prioritizer.sh, check_symbol_fixture_coverage.sh, check_per_symbol_fixture_tests.sh with timestamp restored, rustfmt --check on touched Rust test, git diff --check, targeted rch cargo test, targeted rch clippy, rch cargo check --workspace --all-targets, and workspace clippy/fmt attempts that remain blocked by unrelated existing ABI/core formatting/lint backlog.

close_reason: Implemented per-family fixture coverage threshold artifact and gate. Added tests/conformance/family_coverage_thresholds.v1.json with 40 family threshold records, pass_count=0, fail_count=40, target_total_symbols=4119, target_covered_symbols=391, target_uncovered_symbols=3728, target_coverage_pct=9.49, claim_gate_decision=blocked. Snapshot paths: tests/conformance/conformance_coverage_snapshot.v1.json, tests/conformance/symbol_fixture_coverage.v1.json, tests/conformance/per_symbol_fixture_tests.v1.json. Added scripts/generate_family_coverage_thresholds.py with self-tests for positive pass, negative fail, stale snapshot rejection, missing family rows, duplicate symbol rows, deterministic generation, and coverage regression rejection. Added scripts/check_family_coverage_thresholds.sh to regenerate the artifact, compare canonical output, write target/conformance/family_coverage_thresholds.report.json and target/conformance/family_coverage_thresholds.log.jsonl, and validate required log fields trace_id/bead_id/family_id/threshold_id/expected_coverage/actual_coverage/decision/artifact_refs/source_commit/failure_signature. Added crates/frankenlibc-harness/tests/family_coverage_thresholds_test.rs. Threshold rationale: base 80pct target coverage plus direct, isolated, and dual-mode minimums; small, hard-parts, high-workload, and top-prioritizer families get stricter thresholds. Current top gaps requiring fixture beads: math_abi 1246 uncovered, unistd_abi 709, glibc_internal_abi 569, rpc_abi 159, stdlib_abi 138, wchar_abi 128, string_abi 121, pthread_abi 114, stdio_abi 97, io_internal_abi 87. Validated with py_compile, generator --self-test, generator --check, bash -n, check_family_coverage_thresholds.sh, json.tool, rustfmt --check on touched Rust test, git diff --check, UBS on changed files with 0 criticals after cleanup, br dep cycles empty, rch cargo test -p frankenlibc-harness --test family_coverage_thresholds_test, rch cargo clippy -p frankenlibc-harness --test family_coverage_thresholds_test -- -D warnings, and rch cargo check --workspace --all-targets.
